3d mesh to grammar string reconstruction.name: 3D Mesh To Grammar String Reconstruction
3d mesh to grammar string reconstruction.description:
Overview
You are given the 3D triangular mesh of a small unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV). Your task is to recover the grammar string that describes how the vehicle was assembled from primitive components.

Every UAV in this benchmark was constructed by a compositional grammar whose alphabet encodes (a) the identity and size of each component and (b) how components are connected. Your model must invert the 3D geometry back into that symbolic description.

The input for each test row is one perturbed triangular mesh (stored as a NumPy .npz archive) plus two noisy numeric hints (rounded and jittered weight and thrust). The output is a single grammar string per mesh.

This is a 3D-to-sequence reconstruction task with a structured graph-level metric. Simple encoder-decoder models that emit grammar tokens directly from vertex point clouds are a reasonable baseline, but the target is not a plain sequence — it is a small labelled graph, with typed nodes, unordered edges, and a small operator vocabulary between consecutive nodes. Combined with the ~40% label noise on the training set, the aggressive full-3D geometric perturbation applied to every mesh, and the obfuscated grammar alphabet (see below), naive encoder-decoder setups plateau quickly.

Grammar Specification
Each grammar string is a concatenation of two blocks:

A non-empty sequence of component blocks, each of the form *<letter><TYPE_CODE> optionally followed by an operator token: -, +, ++, +++, etc. (any non-empty run of - and/or + characters).

Zero or more connection tokens, each of the form ^<letter1><letter2>.

Token-level regex (predictions must match this):


^(\*[a-z][A-Z]{2}\d(?:[-+]+)?)+(\^[a-z][a-z])*$

Component block: *<letter><TYPE_CODE>

<letter> is a single lowercase ASCII letter az. This is a local, per-UAV identifier for the component. The first component in the string is usually named a, the second b, etc., but the grader does not depend on this exact labelling — only on which letters appear in which connection tokens. Letters are never compared to the ground-truth letters directly; they are resolved to the component's TYPE_CODE before comparison.

<TYPE_CODE> is exactly three characters: two uppercase letters followed by a single digit. The first letter encodes the component family (hub, arm, motor, strut, etc.), the second letter encodes the component sub-type / orientation, and the digit 09 encodes the size bucket (smaller digits = smaller components, larger digits = larger components). The exact letter-pair alphabet used in this challenge has been deterministically remapped at prepare-time, so it does not match any letter palette you may have seen in public UAV-design datasets. You must learn the vocabulary from the provided train.csv alone.

Operator between consecutive components

The operator token, if present, is one of -, +, ++, +++, etc. (any non-empty run of - and/or + characters, matched by the regex fragment [-+]+). It encodes the relative orientation of the next component with respect to the previous one (roughly: "in-plane", "above", "stacked above", and repeated/combined application thereof). A component block without a trailing operator means the next component attaches at the default orientation. Each complete run of -+ is treated as a single operator token by the grader. Operators contribute 10% of the final score, so getting the broad mix of operator tokens right matters while exact per-position alignment does not.

Connection token: ^<letter1><letter2>

Each ^xy token declares an unordered undirected edge between the component labelled x and the component labelled y. Connections appear at the end of the string, after all component blocks. The order in which connection tokens are listed is irrelevant.

Example (illustrative only; the actual alphabet in train.csv is different):


*aAA0-*bRA3++*cAR1++*dGA3*eAG1^ab^ac^ad^ae

Parsed:

5 components: (a, AA0), (b, RA3), (c, AR1), (d, GA3), (e, AG1).

3 operators: -, ++, ++.

4 connections: {a,b}, {a,c}, {a,d}, {a,e} — component a is connected to every other component (a typical "hub plus four arms" topology).

Evaluation
Submissions are scored with a composite, structured metric that compares the graph-level content of the predicted grammar string to the ground truth. The grader parses both the predicted and the ground-truth grammar_string into four objects:

Node multiset. The multiset of TYPE_CODE values across all parsed components. Per-UAV letter labels are NOT compared.

Typed edge multiset. For every connection ^xy, the grader looks up the TYPE_CODE of components x and y in the predicted grammar and records the sorted pair (TYPE_CODE_x, TYPE_CODE_y). These typed pairs are compared as a multiset. This makes the metric invariant to arbitrary letter relabellings inside the predicted string.

Operator multiset. The multiset of non-empty operator tokens (each a non-empty run of - and/or + characters, e.g. -, +, ++, +++) between consecutive components.

Grammar validity. 1.0 if the predicted string matches the grammar regex above AND contains at least one component; otherwise 0.0.

Each of the three multisets is compared with multiset Jaccard (intersection size over union size; 1.0 if both are empty).

Per-row score:


Row = 0.40 * NodeJaccard

    + 0.40 * EdgeTypedJaccard

    + 0.10 * OperatorJaccard

    + 0.10 * GrammarValidity

Final score is the arithmetic mean of Row scores across all test rows, clipped to [0, 1]. Grading direction is Maximize. Minimum 0.0, Maximum 1.0.

Submissions that score exactly 0.0 automatically (to match grade.py):

the submission is missing the image_id column or the grammar_string column (extra columns are ignored);

image_id contains any duplicate value;

the submission has a different row count than the hidden answers file;

the set of image_id values does not exactly match the set of image_id values in the answers file (equivalently, any test image_id is missing or any unknown image_id is present);

any grammar_string value is NaN / missing (non-string scalar values that are not NaN, e.g. numeric 0, are tolerated: the grader coerces them with astype(str) before scoring, but they will parse as an invalid grammar and contribute 0 on every sub-score);

any uncaught exception is raised during grading.

Note that grade.py uses issubset for column checks, so including additional helper columns beyond image_id and grammar_string does not trigger a zero; they are simply ignored.

Dataset
After the prepare script runs, solvers see the following files in public/:

train/<image_id>.npz — for each training image_id, a NumPy archive with keys vertices (float32 array, shape (N, 3), vertex positions) and faces (int32 array, shape (M, 3), triangle indices into vertices).

test/<image_id>.npz — same format, for test rows. No ground-truth label is provided.

train.csv — four columns: image_id, grammar_string, noisy_weight_lb, noisy_thrust_lb. Approximately 1,120 rows.

test.csv — three columns: image_id, noisy_weight_lb, noisy_thrust_lb. Approximately 280 rows.

sample_submission.csv — two columns: image_id, grammar_string. All predicted strings are a trivial placeholder. The file is a valid submission (correct columns, correct row count) but scores very low.

In private/ (not visible to solvers): answers.csv — image_id, grammar_string. Ground truth for the test set.

Training data properties solvers should know:

image_id is a seeded permutation of 0..N-1 and is unrelated to any upstream mesh index.

Every mesh has been irreversibly perturbed at prepare-time: a full 3D rotation about a random axis by an angle in roughly [-90°, +90°] (so meshes are NOT presented in any canonical axis-aligned frame), a uniform scale in [0.75, 1.25], additive Gaussian vertex jitter with sigma = 0.020 * bbox_diag, and random 15–30% vertex dropout (with face indices rewritten and dangling faces removed). Byte-level and vertex-hash matching against any external mesh corpus will not recover training labels, and pose-normalisation heuristics that assume an upright/forward canonical frame will not work.

The grammar alphabet has been deterministically remapped at prepare-time. The uppercase letters and digits that make up each TYPE_CODE are a seeded bijection over the full A–Z and 0–9 sets, so the token palette you see in train.csv is disjoint from any token palette used by any upstream public vehicle-design dataset. You must learn the vocabulary from train.csv alone.

noisy_weight_lb is rounded to the nearest 5 lb and jittered by ±25% Gaussian. noisy_thrust_lb is rounded to the nearest 25 lb and jittered by ±25%. Test-set noise uses the same distribution. These hints are deliberately weak — they convey rough order-of-magnitude information about the vehicle only, and a retrieval/nearest-neighbour solution over these two scalars alone will not generalise.

Approximately 40% of train-set grammar_string labels carry between 1 and 3 stacked local corruptions (independently drawn from: flipped size digit, flipped type-code letter, dropped edge, added spurious edge, swapped edge endpoint). Test labels and private/answers.csv are clean. This noise simulates realistic annotator disagreement and caps the achievable score of pure memorisation or naive-retrieval approaches well below the theoretical ceiling.

Different meshes may share the same grammar_string. Many UAV topologies (for example, "hub plus four identical arms") recur across the population. Two distinct training rows can have identical grammar_string values but different 3D meshes, different weights, and different thrust values. image_id is unique, and no image_id appears in both train and test.

Submission
Submit a single CSV file that must contain at least the following two columns (extra columns are permitted and will be ignored by the grader):

| Column | Type | Description |

|------------------|--------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|

| image_id | int | Row identifier from test.csv (one row per image_id; no duplicates). |

| grammar_string | string | Predicted grammar string for that mesh. Should satisfy the regex above. |

Requirements (enforced by grade.py):

Header row present. The two required column names must appear exactly as image_id and grammar_string (case-sensitive). Additional columns beyond these two do not cause rejection (the grader uses issubset for the column check) but are not used.

The set of image_id values must be exactly equal to the set of image_id values in test.csv (approximately 280 rows). Any missing or unknown image_id forces the final score to 0.0.

Row count must equal the number of rows in the hidden answers file. Duplicate image_id values force the final score to 0.0.

grammar_string values must be non-NaN. Non-string scalar values that are not NaN are tolerated (the grader coerces them via astype(str)), but anything that does not parse into nodes, edges, and operator tokens under the grammar regex will score 0 on every sub-score for that row. Empty strings and malformed strings are accepted (no rejection) but score 0 on Validity and typically 0 on the Jaccard terms too.

Example submission rows (illustrative — the actual alphabet in train.csv will differ because of the prepare-time remap):


image_id,grammar_string

0,*aAA0-*bRA3++*cAR1++*dGA3*eAG1^ab^ac^ad^ae

1,*aAA1*bRA0^ab

2,*aAA0-*bAA0-*cAA0-*dAA0^ab^ac^ad


 
Expected Output
Output to ./working/submission.csv

# Solver Context

You are working on the challenge `3D Mesh To Grammar String Reconstruction`.

## Goal
Build a real Machine Learning solution that can generalize to unseen
data. Focus on clean, reusable code and a notebook that can run on
Google Colab with A100 hardware.

## Required outputs
- Main notebook: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Mesh To Grammar String Reconstruction/notebook.ipynb`
- Working directory for experiments: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Mesh To Grammar String Reconstruction/working`
- Dataset directory: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Mesh To Grammar String Reconstruction/dataset`
- Repacked Colab archive: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Mesh To Grammar String Reconstruction/dataset.zip`

## Challenge description
Overview
You are given the 3D triangular mesh of a small unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV). Your task is to recover the grammar string that describes how the vehicle was assembled from primitive components.

Every UAV in this benchmark was constructed by a compositional grammar whose alphabet encodes (a) the identity and size of each component and (b) how components are connected. Your model must invert the 3D geometry back into that symbolic description.

The input for each test row is one perturbed triangular mesh (stored as a NumPy .npz archive) plus two noisy numeric hints (rounded and jittered weight and thrust). The output is a single grammar string per mesh.

This is a 3D-to-sequence reconstruction task with a structured graph-level metric. Simple encoder-decoder models that emit grammar tokens directly from vertex point clouds are a reasonable baseline, but the target is not a plain sequence — it is a small labelled graph, with typed nodes, unordered edges, and a small operator vocabulary between consecutive nodes. Combined with the ~40% label noise on the training set, the aggressive full-3D geometric perturbation applied to every mesh, and the obfuscated grammar alphabet (see below), naive encoder-decoder setups plateau quickly.

Grammar Specification
Each grammar string is a concatenation of two blocks:

A non-empty sequence of component blocks, each of the form *<letter><TYPE_CODE> optionally followed by an operator token: -, +, ++, +++, etc. (any non-empty run of - and/or + characters).

Zero or more connection tokens, each of the form ^<letter1><letter2>.

Token-level regex (predictions must match this):


^(\*[a-z][A-Z]{2}\d(?:[-+]+)?)+(\^[a-z][a-z])*$

Component block: *<letter><TYPE_CODE>

<letter> is a single lowercase ASCII letter az. This is a local, per-UAV identifier for the component. The first component in the string is usually named a, the second b, etc., but the grader does not depend on this exact labelling — only on which letters appear in which connection tokens. Letters are never compared to the ground-truth letters directly; they are resolved to the component's TYPE_CODE before comparison.

<TYPE_CODE> is exactly three characters: two uppercase letters followed by a single digit. The first letter encodes the component family (hub, arm, motor, strut, etc.), the second letter encodes the component sub-type / orientation, and the digit 09 encodes the size bucket (smaller digits = smaller components, larger digits = larger components). The exact letter-pair alphabet used in this challenge has been deterministically remapped at prepare-time, so it does not match any letter palette you may have seen in public UAV-design datasets. You must learn the vocabulary from the provided train.csv alone.

Operator between consecutive components

The operator token, if present, is one of -, +, ++, +++, etc. (any non-empty run of - and/or + characters, matched by the regex fragment [-+]+). It encodes the relative orientation of the next component with respect to the previous one (roughly: "in-plane", "above", "stacked above", and repeated/combined application thereof). A component block without a trailing operator means the next component attaches at the default orientation. Each complete run of -+ is treated as a single operator token by the grader. Operators contribute 10% of the final score, so getting the broad mix of operator tokens right matters while exact per-position alignment does not.

Connection token: ^<letter1><letter2>

Each ^xy token declares an unordered undirected edge between the component labelled x and the component labelled y. Connections appear at the end of the string, after all component blocks. The order in which connection tokens are listed is irrelevant.

Example (illustrative only; the actual alphabet in train.csv is different):


*aAA0-*bRA3++*cAR1++*dGA3*eAG1^ab^ac^ad^ae

Parsed:

5 components: (a, AA0), (b, RA3), (c, AR1), (d, GA3), (e, AG1).

3 operators: -, ++, ++.

4 connections: {a,b}, {a,c}, {a,d}, {a,e} — component a is connected to every other component (a typical "hub plus four arms" topology).

Evaluation
Submissions are scored with a composite, structured metric that compares the graph-level content of the predicted grammar string to the ground truth. The grader parses both the predicted and the ground-truth grammar_string into four objects:

Node multiset. The multiset of TYPE_CODE values across all parsed components. Per-UAV letter labels are NOT compared.

Typed edge multiset. For every connection ^xy, the grader looks up the TYPE_CODE of components x and y in the predicted grammar and records the sorted pair (TYPE_CODE_x, TYPE_CODE_y). These typed pairs are compared as a multiset. This makes the metric invariant to arbitrary letter relabellings inside the predicted string.

Operator multiset. The multiset of non-empty operator tokens (each a non-empty run of - and/or + characters, e.g. -, +, ++, +++) between consecutive components.

Grammar validity. 1.0 if the predicted string matches the grammar regex above AND contains at least one component; otherwise 0.0.

Each of the three multisets is compared with multiset Jaccard (intersection size over union size; 1.0 if both are empty).

Per-row score:


Row = 0.40 * NodeJaccard

    + 0.40 * EdgeTypedJaccard

    + 0.10 * OperatorJaccard

    + 0.10 * GrammarValidity

Final score is the arithmetic mean of Row scores across all test rows, clipped to [0, 1]. Grading direction is Maximize. Minimum 0.0, Maximum 1.0.

Submissions that score exactly 0.0 automatically (to match grade.py):

the submission is missing the image_id column or the grammar_string column (extra columns are ignored);

image_id contains any duplicate value;

any uncaught exception is raised during grading.

3d spatial reasoning challenge.name: 3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge
3d spatial reasoning challenge.description:
Overview
3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge is a 3D spatial-reasoning benchmark on real indoor rooms. Each task pairs an indoor point cloud (a 3D reconstruction of a real office, bedroom, kitchen, etc.) with an observer's 3D position and orientation, a natural-language situation description ("I am sitting on a chair facing the table on the other side of the room with a whiteboard to my right"), and a spatial question ("how many chairs are on my immediate left?"). The model must output the correct free-form text answer.

The underlying corpus is a human-annotated collection of situated 3D question-answer pairs grounded in real indoor scenes. ORIENT re-splits the raw evaluation-only corpus into a train / test regime that explicitly probes:

in-distribution generalisation (test_iid);
scene transfer (test_held_scene, held out full rooms never seen in training);
viewpoint-rotation robustness (test_rotation, drawn from the Stage-2 rotated copies of questions).
Any modelling approach is welcome: zero-shot prompting of a pretrained multimodal model, fine-tuning of a pretrained point-cloud or VLM backbone, parameter-efficient adaptation (LoRA / adapters), from-scratch training, or retrieval-augmented generation over the training set. The only methods explicitly not allowed are hand-coded rule systems and hard-coded answer lookup tables that bypass model reasoning (see Method Requirements below).

Challenge Characteristics
Domain: 3D indoor-scene spatial reasoning (real rooms reconstructed as point clouds)
Input modality per task:
a .pth point-cloud file (indoor scene reconstruction, ~100k to 300k points: xyz, RGB, semantic index, instance index)
observer position (three floats, x,y,z) and rotation (four-float quaternion _x,_y,_z,_w)
a situation natural-language description
a question natural-language spatial query
Output modality: a free-form text answer string (typically a direction, number, object name, or yes/no)
Modelling freedom: any approach that actually uses the point cloud + observer pose + question is allowed (zero-shot, few-shot, fine-tuning, from-scratch training). Hand-coded rule systems and hard-coded answer lookup tables are not allowed.
Training data: 500 tasks with scenes + observer state + Q/A labels
Test data: 150 held-out tasks across three diagnostic splits (50 test_iid + 50 test_held_scene + 50 test_rotation)
Evaluation: mean unigram token F1 between predicted and ground-truth answer
Dataset
Each task is accompanied by:

Field	Type	Description
scene	string (path)	Relative path to the .pth point-cloud file for the room
situation	string	Natural-language context describing where the observer is facing
question	string	Natural-language spatial question
position	string (x,y,z)	Observer 3D position, comma-separated
rotation	string (x,y,z,w)	Observer quaternion, comma-separated
answer	string	Ground-truth free-form answer (train only)
The point-cloud format
Each .pth file is a torch.load-able tuple of four arrays, all of shape (N, ...) for the same N (~100k–300k per scene):

Index	Shape	Contents
[0]	(N, 3)	xyz coordinates in metres (world frame, z=0 ground plane)
[1]	(N, 3)	RGB colours in [0, 1]
[2]	(N,)	semantic label index
[3]	(N,)	instance index (object id)
Scenes are shared across tasks: many tasks can reference the same .pth file with different observer positions and questions. Point clouds used in training (train_scenes/) and in testing (test_scenes/) are kept in separate folders so that participants do not accidentally load held-out scenes at training time.

Data Files
File	Description	Columns
train.csv	500 training tasks	id, scene, situation, question, position, rotation, answer
train_scenes/	.pth files referenced by train.csv	
test.csv	150 held-out tasks (answer not provided)	id, scene, situation, question, position, rotation
test_scenes/	.pth files referenced by test.csv (incl. held-out rooms)	
sample_submission.csv	Submission format template (random fill)	id, answer
Participants are expected to carve their own validation split out of train.csv if they want one.

Example Row (from train.csv)
id:         train_00000  
scene:      train_scenes/scene0300_00.pth  
situation:  "I am sitting on a chair facing the table and twiddling my thumbs together of boredom because I don't have a monitor on my table."  
question:   "What is the first object to my direct left of me?"  
position:   1.032,0.734,0  
rotation:   0,0,0.996,0.087  
answer:     window  
Test splits
The test set is a shuffled mixture of three diagnostic splits (split labels are hidden from participants):

Split	Tasks	Probes
test_iid	50	in-distribution generalisation
test_held_scene	50	entire rooms held out from training; tests whether the model generalises spatial reasoning to unseen room layouts
test_rotation	50	rotated-viewpoint copies of questions; tests whether the model's answers are consistent under 90° / 180° / 270° rotations of the observer
All test_held_scene tasks reference rooms whose .pth point cloud never appears in training (point-cloud files for held rooms live only in test_scenes/).

Submission Format
Submit a CSV with exactly the columns id, answer. There must be one row per test task and the id column must match test.csv exactly.

id,answer  
test_00000,"window"  
test_00001,"three"  
test_00002,"left"  
Formatting requirements

answer is a free-form string
Empty strings are permitted but score 0
Case, punctuation, and whitespace do not affect scoring (the grader lowercases, strips punctuation, and tokenises on whitespace before computing token F1)
Evaluation: Token F1
Every task is scored by the unigram token F1 between the predicted and ground-truth answer (after lowercasing, punctuation stripping, and whitespace tokenisation). The final benchmark score is the mean per-task token F1 across the test set.

task_score = token_f1(pred_tokens, gt_tokens)  
final_score = mean(task_score over all 1,100 test tasks)  
Method Requirements
Use whatever modelling approach works best. A strong submission should report held-out token F1 per split and discuss scene-transfer and rotation-robustness behaviour.

Allowed

Zero-shot or few-shot prompting of pretrained multimodal models (VLMs, LLMs that accept point-cloud or text-rendered scene descriptions, pretrained point-cloud backbones like PointNet++, PointTransformer, PointCLIP, etc.)
Full fine-tuning or parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, adapters, prefix-tuning) of a pretrained backbone on train.csv
From-scratch training of a point-cloud + text model on train.csv
Retrieval-augmented generation over train.csv
Learned geometric feature extractors (nearest-object detection, floor-plan extraction, semantic-class histograms) composed with any of the above
Data augmentation (random 3D rotations around Z, xyz jitter, question paraphrasing using training data only)
Ensembles of any of the above
Not allowed

Rule-based or hardcoded solutions (e.g. hand-crafted if/else trees, precomputed answer lookup tables, or any pipeline that does not actually consume the point cloud + observer pose + question as a learned model).
 

What Not To Use
Rule-based or hardcoded solutions (e.g. hand-crafted if/else trees, precomputed answer lookup tables, or any pipeline that does not actually consume the point cloud + observer pose + question as a learned model).

extra_files.solution.py:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge — Solution v9
Pure Deep Learning: Flan-T5-Large + Neutral Ego-Centric Features

Architecture:
- Flan-T5-Large (770M, encoder-decoder) — full fine-tune, batch=16, 12 epochs
- Neutral ego-centric scene description (no name mining, no lookup tables)
- Objects labeled as obj_1, obj_2, ... with raw features (sem_id, color, size, distance, direction)
- The LLM learns object identity purely from training signal
- 4x spatial augmentation via heading rotation (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°)
- No GroupKFold CV (direct training for speed)

Reads from: ./dataset/public/
Outputs to: ./working/submission.csv
"""

import subprocess, sys
def pip_install(*packages):
    subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-q"] + list(packages))

pip_install(
    "torch", "torchvision", "torchaudio",
    "transformers>=4.40.0", "accelerate>=0.34.0",
    "datasets", "scipy", "numpy", "pandas", "sentencepiece", "scikit-learn",
)

import os, math, time, json, csv, re, gc, zipfile, pickle, random
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import torch
from collections import Counter

print(f"PyTorch: {torch.__version__}, CUDA: {torch.cuda.is_available()}")
if torch.cuda.is_available():
    print(f"GPU: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(0)}")

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Configuration
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
DATASET_DIR = "./dataset/public"
TRAIN_CSV   = os.path.join(DATASET_DIR, "train.csv")
TEST_CSV    = os.path.join(DATASET_DIR, "test.csv")
OUTPUT_DIR  = "./working"
SUBMISSION  = os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, "submission.csv")
MODEL_SAVE  = os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, "t5_spatial_v8")

MODEL_ID    = "google/flan-t5-large"
MAX_INPUT   = 512
MAX_OUTPUT  = 32
INFER_BATCH = 16
MAX_OBJ     = 15
SEED        = 42

os.makedirs(OUTPUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)
random.seed(SEED)
np.random.seed(SEED)
torch.manual_seed(SEED)

# Basic color palette for RGB→name mapping (neutral geometric feature)
COLOR_PALETTE = [
    ("white",  np.array([255,255,255])), ("black",  np.array([20,20,20])),
    ("gray",   np.array([128,128,128])), ("red",    np.array([200,50,50])),
    ("green",  np.array([50,180,50])),   ("blue",   np.array([50,50,200])),
    ("brown",  np.array([139,90,43])),   ("beige",  np.array([210,185,155])),
    ("yellow", np.array([230,220,50])),  ("orange", np.array([230,140,30])),
]

STRUCTURAL_SEMS = {0, 1, 2, 22}  # wall, floor, ceiling — filter noise


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. Point Cloud Loading & Instance Extraction
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def load_point_cloud(path):
    """Load .pth point cloud: xyz (N,3), rgb (N,3), sem (N,), inst (N,)."""
    with zipfile.ZipFile(path, 'r') as z:
        with z.open('archive/data.pkl') as pf:
            data = pickle.load(pf, encoding='latin1')
    xyz  = np.array(data[0], dtype=np.float32)
    rgb  = np.array(data[1], dtype=np.float32)
    sem  = np.array(data[2], dtype=np.int64)
    inst = np.array(data[3], dtype=np.int64)
    if rgb.max() <= 1.0:
        rgb *= 255.0  # Normalize to 0-255
    return xyz, rgb, sem, inst


def nearest_color(rgb_mean):
    """Map mean RGB vector to nearest basic color name."""
    best_name, best_dist = "gray", float('inf')
    for name, ref in COLOR_PALETTE:
        d = np.sqrt(np.sum((rgb_mean - ref)**2))
        if d < best_dist:
            best_dist, best_name = d, name
    return best_name


def extract_instances(path, min_points=20):
    """Extract object instances from point cloud with neutral geometric features.

    Returns list of dicts with:
    - id: instance index from point cloud
    - sem: semantic label index (raw integer)
    - centroid: 3D center (x, y, z)
    - height: vertical extent (meters)
    - width: horizontal extent (meters)
    - color: nearest basic color name
    - n_pts: point count (proxy for object size)
    """
    xyz, rgb, sem, inst = load_point_cloud(path)
    objects = []
    for iid in np.unique(inst):
        if iid == -100:
            continue
        mask = inst == iid
        if mask.sum() < min_points:
            continue
        pts = xyz[mask]
        extent = pts.max(0) - pts.min(0)  # (dx, dy, dz)
        objects.append({
            'id':       int(iid),
            'sem':      int(np.bincount(sem[mask].astype(np.int64)).argmax()),
            'centroid': pts.mean(0),
            'height':   float(extent[2]),          # z-extent
            'width':    float(max(extent[0], extent[1])),  # max horizontal
            'color':    nearest_color(rgb[mask].mean(0)),
            'n_pts':    int(mask.sum()),
        })
    return objects


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. Ego-Centric Spatial Feature Extraction
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def parse_pose(pos_str, rot_str):
    """Parse observer position and heading from CSV strings."""
    pos = np.array([float(x) for x in pos_str.split(',')])
    qx, qy, qz, qw = [float(x) for x in rot_str.split(',')]
    heading = 2.0 * math.atan2(qz, qw)  # Yaw from quaternion
    return pos, heading


def compute_ego_relations(objects, obs_pos, heading, exclude_structural=True):
    """Compute ego-centric direction and distance for each object.

    Uses linear algebra:
    - Forward vector from heading angle
    - Left vector = perpendicular to forward
    - Project object→observer vector onto forward/left

    Returns sorted list by distance (closest first).
    """
    fwd = np.array([math.cos(heading), math.sin(heading)])
    lft = np.array([-math.sin(heading), math.cos(heading)])

    relations = []
    for obj in objects:
        if exclude_structural and obj['sem'] in STRUCTURAL_SEMS:
            continue

        # 2D displacement from observer to object
        delta = obj['centroid'][:2] - obs_pos[:2]
        dist = float(np.linalg.norm(delta))

        # Project onto ego-centric axes
        proj_fwd = float(np.dot(delta, fwd))
        proj_lft = float(np.dot(delta, lft))

        # Compute angle and classify direction
        angle = math.degrees(math.atan2(proj_lft, proj_fwd))
        abs_angle = abs(angle)
        if abs_angle <= 30:
            direction = "front"
        elif abs_angle <= 60:
            direction = "front-left" if angle > 0 else "front-right"
        elif abs_angle <= 120:
            direction = "left" if angle > 0 else "right"
        elif abs_angle <= 150:
            direction = "behind-left" if angle > 0 else "behind-right"
        else:
            direction = "behind"

        relations.append({
            'obj': obj,
            'dist': dist,
            'direction': direction,
        })

    # Sort by distance: closest objects first
    relations.sort(key=lambda r: r['dist'])
    return relations


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4. Neutral Scene Text Serialization
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def serialize_scene_neutral(relations, max_obj=MAX_OBJ):
    """Convert ego-centric relations to a neutral text string.

    Format: "obj_1 [sem=5] red h=0.9m 2.0m front | obj_2 [sem=8] brown h=1.5m 1.5m right"

    - Objects are labeled neutrally (obj_1, obj_2, ...)
    - Semantic label is included as a raw integer feature
    - Color, height, distance, direction are geometric features
    - The LLM must learn to map these features to object identities

    No name mining, no lookup tables, no hardcoding.
    """
    parts = []
    for i, rel in enumerate(relations[:max_obj]):
        obj = rel['obj']
        part = (
            f"obj_{i+1} [sem={obj['sem']}] {obj['color']} "
            f"h={obj['height']:.1f}m w={obj['width']:.1f}m "
            f"{rel['dist']:.1f}m {rel['direction']}"
        )
        parts.append(part)

    if not parts:
        return "no objects nearby"
    return " | ".join(parts)


def build_t5_input(scene_text, situation, question):
    """Build T5 input with task prefix for conditional generation."""
    return (
        f"spatial reasoning: "
        f"scene: {scene_text} "
        f"situation: {situation} "
        f"question: {question}"
    )


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 5. Spatial Data Augmentation (Heading Rotation)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def augment_rotations(examples, scene_cache, dataset_dir):
    """Generate 3 additional rotated views for each training example.

    Rotates the observer's heading by 90°, 180°, 270° and recomputes
    all ego-centric spatial features. This teaches the model rotation
    invariance — a key requirement of the challenge.

    Only the scene description changes; situation, question, and answer
    remain the same (the spatial relationships rotate accordingly).
    """
    augmented = []
    rotation_offsets = [
        math.radians(90),
        math.radians(180),
        math.radians(270),
    ]

    for ex in examples:
        for offset in rotation_offsets:
            new_heading = ex['heading'] + offset
            # Recompute ego relations with rotated heading
            rels = compute_ego_relations(
                scene_cache[ex['sfile']], ex['pos'], new_heading
            )
            scene_text = serialize_scene_neutral(rels)
            inp = build_t5_input(scene_text, ex['situation'], ex['question'])
            augmented.append({
                'input': inp,
                'target': ex['target'],
                'scene': ex['scene'],
            })

    return augmented


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 6. Data Preparation
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def prepare_data(train_csv, dataset_dir):
    """Load training data and prepare T5 inputs with augmentation."""
    with open(train_csv, 'r') as f:
        rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))

    scene_cache = {}
    base_examples = []  # Store metadata for augmentation

    for row in rows:
        sfile = row['scene'].split('/')[-1]
        spath = os.path.join(dataset_dir, row['scene'])

        if sfile not in scene_cache:
            try:
                scene_cache[sfile] = extract_instances(spath)
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"  Warning: {sfile}: {e}")
                continue

        pos, heading = parse_pose(row['position'], row['rotation'])
        rels = compute_ego_relations(scene_cache[sfile], pos, heading)
        scene_text = serialize_scene_neutral(rels)
        inp = build_t5_input(scene_text, row['situation'], row['question'])

        base_examples.append({
            'input': inp,
            'target': row['answer'],
            'scene': row['scene'],
            # Metadata for augmentation
            'sfile': sfile,
            'pos': pos,
            'heading': heading,
            'situation': row['situation'],
            'question': row['question'],
        })

    print(f"  Base examples: {len(base_examples)}")

    # Generate rotated augmentations (3 rotations × N examples)
    aug = augment_rotations(base_examples, scene_cache, dataset_dir)
    print(f"  Augmented examples: {len(aug)}")

    # Combine: original + 3 rotations = 4x data
    all_data = []
    for ex in base_examples:
        all_data.append({
            'input': ex['input'],
            'target': ex['target'],
            'scene': ex['scene'],
        })
    all_data.extend(aug)
    random.shuffle(all_data)

    print(f"  Total training examples: {len(all_data)} (4x augmentation)")
    return all_data, base_examples, scene_cache


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 7. Token F1 Evaluation Metric
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def compute_token_f1(pred, gt):
    """Compute unigram token F1 between predicted and ground-truth strings."""
    def tokenize(text):
        return re.sub(r'[^\w\s]', '', text.lower()).split()

    pred_tokens = tokenize(pred)
    gt_tokens = tokenize(gt)

    if not pred_tokens and not gt_tokens:
        return 1.0
    if not pred_tokens or not gt_tokens:
        return 0.0

    pred_counts = Counter(pred_tokens)
    gt_counts = Counter(gt_tokens)
    matches = sum(min(pred_counts[t], gt_counts.get(t, 0)) for t in pred_counts)
    precision = matches / len(pred_tokens)
    recall = matches / len(gt_tokens)
    return 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall) if (precision + recall) > 0 else 0.0



# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 9. Full Training (on all data)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def train_full(all_data):
    """Train final model on all training data."""
    from transformers import (
        T5ForConditionalGeneration, T5Tokenizer,
        Seq2SeqTrainingArguments, Seq2SeqTrainer, DataCollatorForSeq2Seq,
    )
    from datasets import Dataset

    tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
    model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
        MODEL_ID, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16
    ).to("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")

    print(f"  Model params: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())/1e6:.0f}M")

    def preprocess(examples):
        inputs = tokenizer(examples["input"], max_length=MAX_INPUT,
                           truncation=True, padding=False)
        labels = tokenizer(text_target=examples["target"], max_length=MAX_OUTPUT,
                           truncation=True, padding=False)
        inputs["labels"] = labels["input_ids"]
        return inputs

    # Only keep input/target for Dataset
    clean = [{'input': d['input'], 'target': d['target']} for d in all_data]
    dataset = Dataset.from_list(clean)
    dataset = dataset.map(preprocess, batched=True,
                          remove_columns=["input", "target"]).shuffle(seed=SEED)

    collator = DataCollatorForSeq2Seq(tokenizer, model=model, padding=True)

    training_args = Seq2SeqTrainingArguments(
        output_dir=os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, "t5_ckpts"),
        num_train_epochs=12,
        per_device_train_batch_size=16,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=1,
        learning_rate=3e-4,
        lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
        warmup_ratio=0.1,
        bf16=True, logging_steps=10,
        save_strategy="no", seed=SEED,
        report_to="none", remove_unused_columns=False,
        gradient_checkpointing=True,
        gradient_checkpointing_kwargs={"use_reentrant": False},
    )

    trainer = Seq2SeqTrainer(
        model=model, args=training_args, train_dataset=dataset,
        data_collator=collator, processing_class=tokenizer,
    )

    print("  Training Flan-T5-Large (full fine-tune, batch=16, 12 epochs)...")
    trainer.train()
    print("  Done!")

    model.save_pretrained(MODEL_SAVE)
    tokenizer.save_pretrained(MODEL_SAVE)
    print(f"  Model saved to {MODEL_SAVE}")

    del trainer
    gc.collect()
    torch.cuda.empty_cache()
    return model, tokenizer


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 10. Inference
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def run_inference(test_csv, dataset_dir, scene_cache, model, tokenizer):
    """Generate predictions for all test tasks using batched beam search."""
    model.eval()
    device = model.device

    def gen_batch(texts):
        inputs = tokenizer(texts, return_tensors="pt", padding=True,
                           truncation=True, max_length=MAX_INPUT).to(device)
        with torch.inference_mode():
            outputs = model.generate(
                **inputs, max_new_tokens=MAX_OUTPUT,
                num_beams=3, early_stopping=True, no_repeat_ngram_size=2,
            )
        return [tokenizer.decode(o, skip_special_tokens=True).strip()
                for o in outputs]

    with open(test_csv, 'r') as f:
        test_rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))

    all_ids, all_inputs = [], []
    for row in test_rows:
        sfile = row['scene'].split('/')[-1]
        spath = os.path.join(dataset_dir, row['scene'])
        if sfile not in scene_cache:
            try:
                scene_cache[sfile] = extract_instances(spath)
            except:
                all_ids.append(row['id'])
                all_inputs.append(None)
                continue

        pos, heading = parse_pose(row['position'], row['rotation'])
        rels = compute_ego_relations(scene_cache[sfile], pos, heading)
        scene_text = serialize_scene_neutral(rels)
        inp = build_t5_input(scene_text, row['situation'], row['question'])
        all_ids.append(row['id'])
        all_inputs.append(inp)

    preds = {}
    t0 = time.time()
    for bs in range(0, len(all_inputs), INFER_BATCH):
        be = min(bs + INFER_BATCH, len(all_inputs))
        b_ids = all_ids[bs:be]
        b_inputs = all_inputs[bs:be]

        valid = [(i, inp) for i, inp in enumerate(b_inputs) if inp is not None]
        if valid:
            vi, vinp = zip(*valid)
            answers = gen_batch(list(vinp))
            for idx, orig in enumerate(vi):
                preds[b_ids[orig]] = answers[idx]

        for i, inp in enumerate(b_inputs):
            if inp is None:
                preds[b_ids[i]] = ""

        done = be
        if done <= INFER_BATCH or done == len(all_inputs):
            el = time.time() - t0
            print(f"    [{done}/{len(all_inputs)}] {el:.1f}s")

    print(f"  {len(preds)} predictions in {time.time()-t0:.1f}s")
    return preds


# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Main Pipeline
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
def main():
    print("=" * 60)
    print("3D Spatial Reasoning — v9 (Pure ML, Optimized)")
    print("  Flan-T5-Large + Neutral Features + 4x Rotation Augmentation")
    print("=" * 60)

    # Step 1: Data preparation
    print("\n[1/4] Preparing data with 4x rotation augmentation...")
    all_data, base_examples, scene_cache = prepare_data(TRAIN_CSV, DATASET_DIR)

    # Step 2: Full training
    print("\n[2/4] Training (batch=16, 12 epochs)...")
    model, tokenizer = train_full(all_data)

    # Step 3: Inference
    print("\n[3/4] Inference on test set...")
    preds = run_inference(TEST_CSV, DATASET_DIR, scene_cache, model, tokenizer)

    # Step 4: Save submission
    print("\n[4/4] Saving submission...")
    test_df = pd.read_csv(TEST_CSV)
    sub = pd.DataFrame({
        'id': test_df['id'],
        'answer': test_df['id'].map(preds).fillna('')
    })
    assert len(sub) == len(test_df)
    sub.to_csv(SUBMISSION, index=False)

    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"Saved: {SUBMISSION}")
    print(f"  Total: {len(sub)}, Non-empty: {(sub['answer']!='').sum()}")
    print(sub.head(10).to_string(index=False))
    print(f"{'='*60}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
extra_files.solver_context.md:
# Solver Context

You are working on the challenge `3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge`.

## Goal
Build a real Machine Learning solution that can generalize to unseen
data. Focus on clean, reusable code and a notebook that can run on
Google Colab with A100 hardware.

## Required outputs
- Main notebook: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge/notebook.ipynb`
- Working directory for experiments: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge/working`
- Dataset directory: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge/dataset`
- Repacked Colab archive: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge/dataset.zip`

## Challenge description
Overview
3D Spatial Reasoning Challenge is a 3D spatial-reasoning benchmark on real indoor rooms. Each task pairs an indoor point cloud (a 3D reconstruction of a real office, bedroom, kitchen, etc.) with an observer's 3D position and orientation, a natural-language situation description ("I am sitting on a chair facing the table on the other side of the room with a whiteboard to my right"), and a spatial question ("how many chairs are on my immediate left?"). The model must output the correct free-form text answer.

The underlying corpus is a human-annotated collection of situated 3D question-answer pairs grounded in real indoor scenes. ORIENT re-splits the raw evaluation-only corpus into a train / test regime that explicitly probes:

in-distribution generalisation (test_iid);
scene transfer (test_held_scene, held out full rooms never seen in training);
viewpoint-rotation robustness (test_rotation, drawn from the Stage-2 rotated copies of questions).
Any modelling approach is welcome: zero-shot prompting of a pretrained multimodal model, fine-tuning of a pretrained point-cloud or VLM backbone, parameter-efficient adaptation (LoRA / adapters), from-scratch training, or retrieval-augmented generation over the training set. The only methods explicitly not allowed are hand-coded rule systems and hard-coded answer lookup tables that bypass model reasoning (see Method Requirements below).

Challenge Characteristics
Domain: 3D indoor-scene spatial reasoning (real rooms reconstructed as point clouds)
Input modality per task:
a .pth point-cloud file (indoor scene reconstruction, ~100k to 300k points: xyz, RGB, semantic index, instance index)
observer position (three floats, x,y,z) and rotation (four-float quaternion _x,_y,_z,_w)
a situation natural-language description
a question natural-language spatial query
Output modality: a free-form text answer string (typically a direction, number, object name, or yes/no)
Modelling freedom: any approach that actually uses the point cloud + observer pose + question is allowed (zero-shot, few-shot, fine-tuning, from-scratch training). Hand-coded rule systems and hard-coded answer lookup tables are not allowed.
Training data: 500 tasks with scenes + observer state + Q/A labels
Test data: 150 held-out tasks across three diagnostic splits (50 test_iid + 50 test_held_scene + 50 test_rotation)
Evaluation: mean unigram token F1 between predicted and ground-truth answer
Dataset
Each task is accompanied by:

Field	Type	Description
scene	string (path)	Relative path to the .pth point-cloud file for the room
situation	string	Natural-language context describing where the observer is facing
question	string	Natural-language spatial question
position	string (x,y,z)	Observer 3D position, comma-separated
rotation	string (x,y,z,w)	Observer quaternion, comma-separated
answer	string	Ground-truth free-form answer (train only)
The point-cloud format
Each .pth file is a torch.load-able tuple of four arrays, all of shape (N, ...) for the same N (~100k–300k per scene):

Index	Shape	Contents
[0]	(N, 3)	xyz coordinates in metres (world frame, z=0 ground plane)
[1]	(N, 3)	RGB colours in [0, 1]
[2]	(N,)	semantic label index
[3]	(N,)	instance index (object id)
Scenes are shared across tasks: many tasks can reference the same .pth file with different observer positions and questions. Point clouds used in training (train_scenes/) and in testing (test_scenes/) are kept in separate folders so that participants do not accidentally load held-out scenes at training time.

Data Files
File	Description	Columns
train.csv	500 training tasks	id, scene, situation, question, position, rotation, answer
train_scenes/	.pth files referenced by train.csv	
test.csv	150 held-out tasks (answer not provided)	id, scene, situation, question, position, rotation
test_scenes/	.pth files referenced by test.csv (incl. held-out rooms)	
sample_submission.csv	Submission format template (random fill)	id, answer
Participants are expected to carve their own validation split out of train.csv if they want one.

Example Row (from train.csv)
id:         train_00000  
scene:      train_scenes/scene0300_00.pth  
situation:  "I am sitting on a chair facing the table and twiddling my thumbs together of boredom because I don't have a monitor on my table."  
question:   "What is the first object to my direct left of me?"  
position:   1.032,0.734,0  
rotation:   0,0,0.996,0.087  
answer:     window  
Test splits
The test set is a shuffled mixture of three diagnostic splits (split labels are hidden from participants):

Split	Tasks	Probes
test_iid	50	in-distribution generalisation
test_held_scene	50	entire rooms held out from training; tests whether the model generalises spatial reasoning to unseen room layouts
test_rotation	50	rotated-viewpoint copies of questions; tests whether the model's answers are consistent under 90° / 180° / 270° rotations of the observer
All test_held_scene tasks reference rooms whose .pth point cloud never appears in training (point-cloud files for held rooms live only in test_scenes/).

Submission Format
Submit a CSV with exactly the columns id, answer. There must be one row per test task and the id column must match test.csv exactly.

id,answer  
test_00000,"window"  
test_00001,"three"  
test_00002,"left"  
Formatting requirements

answer is a free-form string
Empty strings are permitted but score 0
Case, punctuation, and whitespace do not affect scoring (the grader lowercases, strips punctuation, and tokenises on whitespace before computing token F1)
Evaluation: Token F1
Every task is scored by the unigram token F1 between the predicted and ground-truth answer (after lowercasing, punctuation stripping, and whitespace tokenisation). The final benchmark score is the mean per-task token F1 across the test set.

task_score = token_f1(pred_tokens, gt_tokens)  
final_score = mean(task_score over all 1,100 test tasks)  
Method Requirements
Use whatever modelling approach works best. A strong submission should report held-out token F1 per split and discuss scene-transfer and rotation-robustness behaviour.

Allowed

Zero-shot or few-shot prompting of pretrained multimodal models (VLMs, LLMs that accept point-cloud or text-rendered scene descriptions, pretrained point-cloud backbones like PointNet++, PointTransformer, PointCLIP, etc.)
Full fine-tuning or parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, adapters, prefix-tuning) of a pretrained backbone on train.csv
From-scratch training of a point-cloud + text model on train.csv
Retrieval-augmented generation over train.csv
Learned geometric feature extractors (nearest-object detection, floor-plan extraction, semantic-class histograms) composed with any of the above
Data augmentation (random 3D rotations around Z, xyz jitter, question paraphrasing using training data only)
Ensembles of any of the above
Not allowed

Rule-based or hardcoded solutions (e.g. hand-crafted if/else trees, precomputed answer lookup tables, or any pipeline that does not actually consume the point cloud + observer pose + question as a learned model).
 

What Not To Use
Rule-based or hardcoded solutions (e.g. hand-crafted if/else trees, precomputed answer lookup tables, or any pipeline that does not actually consume the point cloud + observer pose + question as a learned model).

## Solver policy

calibrated cross variant chip region cor.name: Calibrated Cross Variant Chip Region Correspondence
calibrated cross variant chip region cor.description:
Overview
Backside infrared microscopy is commonly used to inspect fabricated chips under different acquisition settings. This challenge focuses on a local region-correspondence task: given one query infrared crop and four candidate crops from an alternate acquisition variant, identify which candidate shows the same underlying chip region.

The task is intentionally fine-grained. All inputs are local crops rather than full-chip reference images. Some distractors come from the same subdesign but differ in position or scale, while others come from different subdesigns at similar positions. As a result, the benchmark is closer to local structural correspondence under acquisition shift than to ordinary coarse image classification.

Each example includes a public difficulty_tier:

easy: distractors come from different subdesigns only

medium: distractors include one same-subdesign far-position crop, one same-position adjacent-scale crop from the same subdesign, and one same-position adjacent-scale crop from a different subdesign

hard: distractors include two same-subdesign variants with position and scale variation, plus one same-position adjacent-scale crop from a different subdesign

Evaluation
Submissions are scored with:

RawScore = 0.85 Weighted_Accuracy + 0.15 Signed_Confidence_Quality

The final score is clipped to [0.0, 1.0].

Each test example has a difficulty weight:

easy -> 1.0

medium -> 1.5

hard -> 2.0

Weighted_Accuracy is the weighted fraction of examples where correct_match exactly matches the ground-truth label in {A, B, C, D}. Invalid correct_match values are treated as incorrect.

Submissions must also provide score_A, score_B, score_C, and score_D. For each row, the grader:

parses these values as numeric

replaces invalid or missing values with 0.0

clips negative values to 0.0

normalizes the four values to sum to 1

replaces all-nonpositive rows with (0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)

Let p_true be the normalized probability assigned to the true label. The per-example confidence term is:

confidence_quality_i = 1 - (-log(p_true) / log(4))

This value is clipped to [-0.5, 1.0], so overconfident wrong predictions are penalized. The final confidence component is the weighted average of these values using the same difficulty weights.

Dataset
The prepared dataset contains:

public/train.csv

public/test.csv

public/sample_submission.csv

public/dataset_stats.csv

public/images/queries/

public/images/candidates/

Each example contains one query crop and four candidate crops drawn from the alternate acquisition variant of the same chip design family. The candidate set is sample-specific: crops are rendered separately for each example rather than reused as a shared global candidate bank. A row may therefore contain crops from the same subdesign at different positions or scales, as well as crops from different subdesigns.

Files And Columns
public/train.csv contains labeled training examples with:

image_id

difficulty_tier

query_image_path

candidate_A_path

candidate_B_path

candidate_C_path

candidate_D_path

correct_match

public/test.csv contains the same inputs, but correct_match is withheld.

public/sample_submission.csv contains:

image_id

correct_match

score_A

score_B

score_C

score_D

public/dataset_stats.csv stores summary metadata. Query images are stored in public/images/queries/, and candidate images are stored in public/images/candidates/.

Image Properties And Scale
All query and candidate images are 224 x 224 RGB PNG files. Each example contains exactly five images: one query crop and four candidate crops.

The crops are generated with deterministic position jitter, scale variation, and visual perturbations. Query and candidate images may differ in blur, brightness, contrast, sharpness, small rotation, and partial rectangular occlusion. Query images receive stronger perturbations than candidate images so that the task remains challenging without making the candidate set excessively ambiguous.

Dataset scale:

Training examples: 408

Test examples: 192

Total examples: 600

Candidates per example: 4

Difficulty tiers: easy, medium, hard

Submission Format
Submit a CSV at ./working/submission.csv with exactly these columns:

image_id,correct_match,score_A,score_B,score_C,score_D

Example:

CVCM_000409,B,0.10,0.72,0.11,0.07

Submission Requirements
The file must contain one row per test image_id

Every image_id from public/test.csv must be present

Duplicate image_id rows are deduplicated by keeping the first occurrence

After deduplication, the submitted image_id set must exactly match the test set

If the submitted image_id set does not match the test set, the submission receives a score of 0.0

correct_match must be one of A, B, C, or D

score_A, score_B, score_C, and score_D must be parseable as numeric values

Confidence values do not need to sum to 1; the grader will normalize them

Extra columns are ignored by the grader

 

What Not To Use
No lookup tables or deterministic mappings from filenames, hashes, hidden metadata, or candidate-path patterns directly to the correct label.
No hardcoded {image_id -> correct_match}, {query_hash -> correct_match}, or {candidate_hash -> correct_match} mappings.
No tuning, calibration, adapter training, or other adaptation on the withheld test examples. Training and fine-tuning are allowed only on the provided public training split.
No hosted or closed-model API calls at inference time. Local open-weight pretrained vision models are allowed, including pretrained CNNs and vision encoders loaded and run entirely within the execution environment.
No traditional machine-learning models as the primary solving method. This includes logistic regression, SVMs, random forests, gradient boosting, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, k-NN, or similar models trained on handcrafted image features or pairwise similarity features.
No classical algorithmic or heuristic-only matching pipelines as the primary solving method. This includes template matching, perceptual hashing, keypoint-matching pipelines such as SIFT, ORB, or AKAZE, pixel or edge similarity ranking, handcrafted rule systems, or any other non-neural matching pipeline used as the main decision mechanism.
Classical preprocessing is allowed only as a minor supporting step. The core prediction logic must come from a learned vision model rather than a traditional ML model, rule-based system, or handcrafted matching algorithm.
No attempts to exploit grader or submission-format behavior.

channel robust rare event detection on d.name: Channel Robust Rare Event Detection On Difference Imaging
channel robust rare event detection on d.description:
Overview
Wide-field optical transient surveys scan the night sky continuously and process the incoming exposures through a difference-imaging pipeline, which aligns each new frame against a deep historical co-add of the same patch of sky and subtracts one from the other. Every local brightness change detected by this pipeline produces a candidate, accompanied by a three-channel cutout: the reference co-add, the current science frame, and the difference image. Most candidates are artefacts — subtraction residuals, cosmic rays, unmasked bad pixels — and only roughly one in a thousand is a genuine astrophysical transient worth following up.

The cutouts in this challenge originate from an operational transient survey's nightly pipeline, running on a 16-chip CMOS mosaic at a 1-metre-class optical telescope under routine on-sky conditions. Each candidate is a 29×29 pixel, three-channel (reference / science / difference) cutout extracted at the detected position, with pixel values pre-normalised to [0, 1] by the survey's native asinh stretch. Every candidate also carries a detector-chip identifier (78 unique chips across the dataset) that records which CMOS sub-quadrant produced it — different chips have different point-spread functions, gains, dark-current profiles, and bad-pixel masks, and a Real/Bogus classifier deployed in front of an alert broker must generalise across them.

Given a 29×29×3 candidate cutout, your model must emit a real/bogus score. Every test candidate is evaluated under four channel-ablation conditions — full, reference masked, science masked, difference masked — so your submission has exactly 4 rows per test candidate, and 1,000 test candidates × 4 conditions = 4,000 total submission rows. The leaderboard score combines per-condition missed-detection rate at a strict 0.5% false-positive rate with a worst-chip held-out-detector term, a channel-ablation gap term, and a small positive-class F1 tail.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored by a composite in [0, 1] where higher is better. The composite is computed as Score = 1 − Penalty with a seven-term weighted penalty:

Score = 1 − ( 0.30 * MDR_full
             + 0.20 * MDR_no_diff
             + 0.10 * MDR_no_ref
             + 0.10 * MDR_no_sci
             + 0.15 * WorstChip_MDR_full
             + 0.10 * ChannelAblationGap
             + 0.05 * (1 − F1_pos_full) )
Each penalty term is independently clipped to [0, 1] before weighting.

For each channel condition, the grader finds the smallest threshold tau_c such that at most 0.5% of Bogus scores in that condition are strictly greater than tau_c, then counts how many Real scores are not strictly greater than tau_c:

MDR_c = 1 − ( fraction of Real candidates in condition c with score > tau_c )
Strict inequality is deliberate — a classifier that outputs the same score for every input saturates every MDR_c at 1.0 rather than 0.0. The remaining terms:

WorstChip_MDR_full — the maximum MDR_full computed independently on each held-out detector chip's own Real+Bogus pool, restricted to chips with at least 3 Real candidates. Measures the worst single-chip generalisation outcome.
ChannelAblationGap = max(0, min(1, (MDR_no_ref + MDR_no_sci) / 2 − MDR_full)). Signed one-sided gap between the average of the two single-channel ablations and the full-channel MDR, clipped to [0, 1]. A model that degrades under reference or science masking pays for it here.
F1_pos_full — positive-class F1 at the MDR_full threshold, computed by hard-thresholding the full-condition scores at tau_full. Included as a sanity tail to prevent degenerate constant-score submissions from scoring well on the MDR terms alone.
Score range: 0.0 to 1.0. Higher is better.

Dataset
public/train.csv: 250 training candidates with noisy binary labels
public/test.csv: 4,000 rows covering 1,000 test candidates × 4 channel-ablation conditions
public/sample_submission.csv: 4,000 rows, a zero-score starter template
public/train_images/: 29×29 RGB PNGs, one per training candidate
public/test_images/: 29×29 RGB PNGs, one per (candidate, condition) pair (so 4,000 PNGs total)
Input columns in train.csv:

candidate_id (string): identifier of the form TRB_xxxxxxxxxxxx
file_name (string): PNG filename in public/train_images/
det_id (int): detector chip identifier (3-digit decimal, 78 unique values total; training and test chip pools are disjoint)
noisy_label (int, 0 or 1): binary Real/Bogus label with a small amount of asymmetric label noise injected — plain binary cross-entropy will converge sub-optimally on this column without a noise-robust training strategy
Input columns in test.csv:

candidate_id (string): identifier matching sample_submission.csv
condition (string): one of full, no_ref, no_sci, no_diff
file_name (string): PNG filename in public/test_images/
det_id (int): detector chip identifier
Columns in sample_submission.csv:

candidate_id (string): identifier matching test.csv
condition (string): one of full, no_ref, no_sci, no_diff
score (float): zero for every row in the starter template; replace with your predicted probability of Real before submitting
Test candidates come from 8 detector chips held out from the training split, so a model must generalise across sensors rather than memorise chip-specific artefacts. Each test image is the channel-ablated cutout for its condition (the named channel zeroed in normalised pixel space), with a small amount of deterministic Gaussian noise added to defeat pixel-exact matching against any upstream mirror.

Submission
Submit a CSV file with one row per (candidate_id, condition) pair.

Required row count: exactly 4,000 = 1,000 test candidates × 4 channel conditions.

Columns:

candidate_id (string): identifier matching test.csv
condition (string): one of full, no_ref, no_sci, no_diff
score (float, [0, 1]): probability of Real; any monotone transform of a calibrated probability is acceptable
Example (two candidates shown, one block of four rows per candidate):

candidate_id,condition,score
TRB_a1b2c3d4e5f6,full,0.94
TRB_a1b2c3d4e5f6,no_ref,0.78
TRB_a1b2c3d4e5f6,no_sci,0.65
TRB_a1b2c3d4e5f6,no_diff,0.12
TRB_d4e5f6g7h8i9,full,0.03
TRB_d4e5f6g7h8i9,no_ref,0.05
TRB_d4e5f6g7h8i9,no_sci,0.04
TRB_d4e5f6g7h8i9,no_diff,0.02
Requirements:

Must contain exactly 4,000 rows. Submissions with fewer rows have the missing conditions defaulted to score=0.0, which saturates the MDR for those conditions at 1.0 and collapses the composite toward zero — a 1,000-row submission that covers only the full condition will score near the sample-submission floor regardless of how well the full-condition predictions are tuned.
score must be a finite float in [0, 1]; values outside are clipped, NaN/Inf are treated as 0.0.
condition must be exactly one of the four allowed values.
Duplicate (candidate_id, condition) pairs are silently deduplicated by the grader (first occurrence kept).
Approach Guidelines
This is a rare-positive binary classification task at a strict 0.5% FPR operating point, evaluated under four channel-ablation conditions, across detectors the model has never seen, using training labels that contain asymmetric noise. Three independent robustness axes stack on top of the core classifier.

Approaches that are not permitted:

Downloading external difference-imaging cutout corpora or transient-survey alert archives to recover test labels
Hardcoded {candidate_id → label} lookup tables or perceptual-hash matching against any external mirror
Fine-tuning, LoRA, or adapter updates on the test images
Approaches that are encouraged:

Channel-dropout augmentation during training (zero one of the three channels at random with probability 0.2–0.4)
Noise-robust losses such as Generalised Cross-Entropy, Symmetric Cross-Entropy, or co-teaching on the noisy_label column
Domain-adaptive training across chips (IRM, GroupDRO, CORAL, or chip-conditioned normalisation)
Upsampling the 29×29 input to 64×64 or 96×96 with bilinear interpolation before feeding an ImageNet-pretrained backbone
 

What Not To Use
External difference-imaging cutout corpora or transient-survey alert archives used to recover test labels
Hardcoded {candidate_id → label} lookup tables or perceptual-hash matching against any upstream mirror
Fine-tuning, LoRA, or adapter training on the withheld test images
Pretrained Real/Bogus classifiers from prior astronomy transient surveys
