laser mirror maze exit prediction.name: Laser Mirror Maze Exit Prediction
laser mirror maze exit prediction.description:
Overview
Laser Mirror Maze Exit Prediction is a visual-reasoning-plus-physics benchmark. Each task is a 640x640 PNG showing a square grid populated with two kinds of 45-degree mirrors (/ and \) and a single laser source placed in an interior cell (shown as a red dot with a bold red arrow indicating the beam's initial direction). Critically, the beam's path is deliberately not drawn on the image; the participant must mentally trace the ray through every mirror it encounters and determine the boundary cell at which it leaves the grid. The model must predict the integer index of that exit cell under a fixed edge * N + position encoding (see below).

The challenge is a compact, procedurally generated test of visual-plus-symbolic physics reasoning. Given only the rendered pixels, a solver must identify each mirror's orientation, locate the laser source and its initial direction, and trace the beam through every reflection to determine the cell at which it exits the grid. Because no trajectory is drawn, the task is genuinely hard: even modest 12x12 grids can require twenty or more reflections, and any small mis-identification of a mirror orientation or the source direction compounds catastrophically along the beam. The test set is split into five diagnostic axes that probe whether the learner has acquired genuine spatial-plus-physics reasoning rather than surface-level pattern matching over the 12x12 training grids.

Challenge Characteristics
Domain: synthetic, procedurally generated laser-in-a-mirror-maze puzzles
Input modality: a single 640x640 RGB PNG per task
Output modality: a single real-valued number. The ground truth is a non-negative integer in [0, 4*N - 1] (with N the grid size shown in the image); integer predictions are typical but continuous predictions are also accepted and graded by squared error
Training data: 8,900 tasks with images and ground-truth integers
Test data: 1,500 held-out tasks across five diagnostic splits
Evaluation: mean squared error between the predicted and ground-truth exit index. Lower is better; range [0, +inf). Malformed (non-numeric, NaN, infinite, or missing) predictions are assigned a fixed finite per-row penalty of (4*N - 1)^2
Dataset
Every task is delivered as a 640x640 RGB image with the following layout:

+----------------------------------------+  
| where does the laser exit?             |  
|  [source]  [/  mirror]  [\  mirror]    |  
|  0  1  2  3  4  5  ...    N-1          |  
| 0 +--+--+--+--+--+--+ ...              |  
|   |  | /|  |\ |  |  |                  |  
| 1 +--+--+--+--+--+--+                  |  
|   |  |  | @>  |  |  |  (@=source)      |  
| 2 +--+--+--+--+--+--+                  |  
|   |\ |  |  |  |  | /|                  |  
| ...   (beam path NOT drawn, infer it)  |  
| N-1 +--+--+--+--+--+--+                |  
+----------------------------------------+  
The grid is drawn with a subtle two-tone checkerboard background and thin grey grid lines; the outer border is bold. Row and column indices (0 through N-1) are printed along the top and left edges for disambiguation.
Mirrors are drawn as short gold diagonal line segments with a lighter highlight underneath. / mirrors run from lower-left to upper-right; \ mirrors run from upper-left to lower-right.
The laser source is a bright red dot on a soft red halo, with a bold red arrow extending roughly one full cell in the beam's initial direction (one of N, E, S, W). The arrow is the participant's only visual cue for the initial heading of the beam.
The beam's traced path is not drawn. The participant must mentally simulate the reflection-by-reflection trajectory from source to exit.
The training distribution uses N = 12 grids with mirror density between 18 and 28 percent. The five diagnostic test splits each shift one axis of generalisation: an in-distribution baseline; denser mirrors (45-60 percent); larger grids (N = 18); longer beam paths (bounces >= 18); and a hardest combination of all three. Split labels are not exposed in the public test CSV.

Data Files
File	Description	Columns
train.csv	8,900 training tasks	id, image, answer
train_images/	8,900 task PNGs (640x640)	
test.csv	1,500 held-out test tasks	id, image
test_images/	1,500 test PNGs	
sample_submission.csv	Submission format template	id, answer
The image column is a relative path of the form train_images/train_0000000.png. Filenames are unique within each split. Participants are expected to carve their own validation split out of train.csv if they want one.

Example Row (from train.csv)
id:      train_0000000  
image:   train_images/train_0000000.png  
answer:  45  
A train.csv answer of 45 on a 12x12 grid decodes as edge = 45 // 12 = 3 (LEFT) and position = 45 % 12 = 9, i.e. the beam exits the left edge at row 9.

Exit-index encoding (counting direction)
The exit index is answer = edge * N + position. The four edges are numbered clockwise from the top; positions along each edge are counted in a fixed direction:

edge	integer range	Which edge	position counting
0	[0, N-1]	TOP edge (beam exits through the top)	column index, 0 at the left, increasing to the right
1	[N, 2N-1]	RIGHT edge (beam exits through the right)	row index, 0 at the top, increasing downward
2	[2N, 3N-1]	BOTTOM edge (beam exits through the bottom)	column index, 0 at the left, increasing to the right
3	[3N, 4N-1]	LEFT edge (beam exits through the left)	row index, 0 at the top, increasing downward
In every case position is the index of the last in-grid cell the beam was in before leaving the grid, read in the direction specified. The same convention is used for every grid size: training grids use N = 12 (answers in [0, 47]) and the held-out test_larger / test_hard splits use N = 18 (answers in [0, 71]), so the model must infer N from the rendered image.

Submission Format
Submit a CSV with exactly the columns id and answer. There must be one row per test task and the id column must match test.csv exactly.

id,answer  
test_000000,17  
test_000001,48  
test_000002,3  
Formatting requirements

Each answer must be a real number. Integer predictions in [0, 4*N - 1] are the natural choice (the ground truth is always an integer in that range), but continuous real-valued predictions anywhere on the real line are also accepted and scored by squared error.
Missing, non-numeric, NaN, or infinite answers are treated as malformed and receive a fixed finite per-row penalty of (4*N - 1)^2.
Valid numeric predictions are NOT clipped. A prediction outside [0, 4*N - 1] (including negative values or values much larger than the answer space) is graded by its raw squared error against the ground truth, so the squared error grows quadratically with distance from the true answer.
Evaluation: Mean Squared Error
The primary metric is mean squared error between the predicted and ground-truth exit indices, averaged over all test tasks:

mse = mean( (pred_i - true_i)^2  for i in test )  
Lower is better. A perfect submission scores 0.0. Because valid numeric predictions are accepted without clipping, the per-row squared error is in principle unbounded: a prediction arbitrarily far from the ground truth yields an arbitrarily large error (and hence an arbitrarily large mean). In practice, a sensible predictor that outputs values near the answer space [0, 4*N - 1] has per-row squared error bounded by (4*N - 1)^2, and this same value is used as the fixed finite per-row penalty for malformed predictions.

The edge * N + position encoding means that two predictions differing by 1 often correspond to neighbouring cells along the same edge (error = 1), while a prediction that picks the wrong edge entirely typically differs by roughly N, yielding an error on the order of N^2. The metric therefore strongly rewards correct-edge predictions even when the exact position is slightly off, and strongly punishes wrong-edge predictions.

Method Requirements
Laser Mirror Maze Exit Prediction is a learning-centric benchmark. The intended solution space is any learned approach that takes the rendered image as input and produces an integer. Strong submissions should:

Build a learner that reads the pixels and predicts the correct exit index for each task;
Report held-out MSE broken down by the self-carved validation subset it used, and discuss how its approach is expected to generalise to the hidden diagnostic axes (larger grids, denser mirrors, longer beam paths);
Use only the supplied training data for parameter fitting and hyperparameter selection.
Prohibited approaches

Rule-based or hand-coded solutions that reconstruct the grid, identify every mirror by colour/orientation, and simulate the ray-tracing explicitly. The benchmark measures what a learning pipeline can acquire from the visual input, not the author's ability to re-implement the generator.
Hardcoded lookup tables indexed by the visible mirror pattern, the rendered image hash, or any manually-enumerated exit outcome.
Test-time fine-tuning, retrieval over the test set, or per-task adaptation using the test labels.
Allowed

Any learned image-to-integer pipeline, including end-to-end CNN or ViT regressors/classifiers, image-to-sequence models that emit the integer as a short number string, graph neural networks over detected grid cells, pretrained vision backbones fine-tuned on the training set, or prompted vision-language models;
Curriculum learning, data augmentation (colour jitter, small rotations, crops within the grid), auxiliary objectives, or multi-head training that predicts the exit index alongside other structural labels (e.g. the exit edge as a four-way classification head), provided only the supplied training data is used to fit parameters;
Ensembling of multiple learned models trained on the supplied training set.
 

Expected Output
Output to ./working/submission.csv

load-bearing counterfactual localization.name: Load-Bearing Counterfactual Localization
load-bearing counterfactual localization.description:
Overview
Real inspection systems often fail before there is an obvious visual failure. A shelf looks stable, a fixture looks assembled, a block looks harmless, but one small contact zone is carrying the future outcome. This challenge asks you to find that zone.

Each sample is a rendered synthetic mechanical scene before any visible failure occurs. A hidden counterfactual physics check first verifies that the scene is stable. Then candidate supports are removed one at a time and the outcome is evaluated again. The target is the visible support interface whose removal changes the future outcome from stable to failing.

This is a computer vision segmentation task, but the label is not an object category. It is a counterfactual physical role projected back into image space. You must predict a binary mask and bounding box for the load-bearing interface region, not for every object, not for the largest object, and not for the whole support column.

The target is represented as a mask rather than as a list of boxes. If a critical interface appears as separated visible regions, such as a brace touching the load at two edge patches, the mask convention is the union of all counterfactually critical visible interface pixels. The submitted bounding box should be the single tight enclosing box around that full mask, even if the mask has multiple disconnected components.

Why This Is Hard
The target is an interface, not an object. Most of the support object is not part of the target mask. Over-segmenting the full pillar or wedge gives poor Dice and boundary score.

The critical and decoy supports use similar colors and materials. A color threshold or saliency heuristic is intentionally weak.

The test split is a held-out mechanism family. Training contains cantilever_key and platform_wedge; private test contains ramp_wedge.

The label is causal. The correct patch is determined by a hidden remove-and-rerun physics intervention, not by appearance alone.

The masks are small. Small localization errors have a larger effect on Dice, boundary F1, and box IoU than they would in ordinary object segmentation.

Dataset
File descriptions
train.csv -- Labeled training metadata with image paths, interface masks, bounding boxes, critical asset names, and counterfactual summaries.

test.csv -- Unlabeled test metadata with image paths and image dimensions.

sample_submission.csv -- Submission template with random nonzero mask and box predictions.

train/ -- RGB PNG images for 100 training scenes.

test/ -- RGB PNG images for 50 private test scenes.

train_masks/ -- Binary PNG masks for the load-bearing interface in each training scene.

Column descriptions
train.csv columns:

id -- 12-character hashed scene identifier.

image_path -- Relative path to the RGB training image.

interface_mask_path -- Relative path to the binary target interface mask.

template -- Mechanism family used to generate the scene.

critical_asset -- Name of the asset whose removal flips the hidden outcome.

interface_mask_rle -- Run-length encoding of the binary target interface mask.

bbox_xmin, bbox_ymin, bbox_xmax, bbox_ymax -- Pixel-space target bounding box.

baseline_failure -- Whether the original scene failed under simulation. This is False for all released scenes.

critical_removal_failure -- Whether removing the critical asset caused failure. This is True for all released scenes.

counterfactual_summary -- JSON string with baseline, decoy-removal, and critical-removal simulation metrics.

test.csv columns:

id -- 12-character hashed scene identifier.

image_path -- Relative path to the RGB test image.

height -- Image height in pixels.

width -- Image width in pixels.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored with a weighted localization metric in [0, 1], where higher is better.


mask_quality = 0.65  *dice(true_mask, pred_mask) + 0.35*  boundary_f1(true_mask, pred_mask)

bbox_quality = box_iou(true_bbox, pred_bbox)

observed_error = 1.0 - (0.70  *mask_quality + 0.30*  bbox_quality)

calibration = 1.0 - abs(predicted_uncertainty - observed_error)

case_score = 0.55  *mask_quality + 0.30*  bbox_quality + 0.15 * calibration

The final score is the weighted average over test cases. Held-out mechanism-family cases receive slightly higher weight.

Submission
Submit a CSV with exactly one row per id in test.csv.

id -- Test scene identifier.

interface_mask_rle -- RLE-encoded binary mask for all counterfactually critical visible interface pixels. The mask may contain multiple disconnected components.

bbox_xmin -- Predicted minimum x coordinate.

bbox_ymin -- Predicted minimum y coordinate.

bbox_xmax -- Predicted maximum x coordinate.

bbox_ymax -- Predicted maximum y coordinate.

uncertainty_score -- Predicted localization error in [0, 1].

Example:


id,interface_mask_rle,bbox_xmin,bbox_ymin,bbox_xmax,bbox_ymax,uncertainty_score

a7e3d4f19b02,"109981 11 110493 12 111005 12",294,220,374,360,0.18

5c81b20ea6f7,"105224 9 105735 11 106247 11",281,210,367,344,0.24

RLE encoding specification
Convert the predicted 2D mask to binary values {0, 1}.

Flatten in row-major order using numpy.ravel(order="C").

Use 1-based indexing for run starts.

Encode as repeated start length integer pairs separated by spaces.

Empty masks are encoded as an empty string.

If there are multiple predicted critical contact regions, encode them together in one binary mask.

Requirements
The file must contain exactly one row per test id.

Every id from test.csv must appear exactly once with no duplicates.

interface_mask_rle must decode to the exact image shape listed in test.csv.

Bounding box coordinates must be finite numeric values and should enclose all predicted foreground pixels in interface_mask_rle.

uncertainty_score must be finite and in [0, 1]; invalid or missing values are clipped during grading.

File format must be .csv with exact column names: id,interface_mask_rle,bbox_xmin,bbox_ymin,bbox_xmax,bbox_ymax,uncertainty_score.

 

Expected Output
Output to ./working/submission.csv

marketplace listing attribute canonicali.name: Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization
marketplace listing attribute canonicali.description:
Overview
Marketplace catalogs depend on clean, normalized product records for search ranking, deduplication, moderation, and downstream quality systems. In practice, sellers submit noisy drafts with inconsistent casing, shorthand attributes, locale-specific size formats, keyword stuffing, and partially missing structured hints. In this challenge, your task is to generate the canonical marketplace attribute string for each draft listing.

Each row is one seller-created listing draft. The dataset provides the raw seller text (raw_title, raw_bullets, raw_description), marketplace context, and noisy extracted hints. Your output must be a single normalized string in an exact key order, containing 8 canonical attributes: brand, product type, variant, color, size, pack count, material, and condition.

This is a sequence-to-sequence normalization benchmark. Strong solutions should read the raw listing text directly, normalize abbreviations and locale-specific forms, use metadata carefully, and enforce the exact target format. Weak solutions usually trust the hint columns too much or produce free-form text that does not match the required canonical schema.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored using Rarity-Weighted Structured Attribute Accuracy.

For each listing:

Parse the predicted canonical_listing_spec into 8 required key-value fields.
Parse the ground-truth canonical_listing_spec into the same 8 fields.
Compute a weighted partial score and an exact-row indicator, then blend:
row_score = 0.70 × weighted_partial + 0.30 × exact_row_match  
Where:

weighted_partial = Σ FIELD_WEIGHTS[k] × (pred_k == truth_k) across all 8 fields; weights sum to 1.0
exact_row_match = 1.0 if all 8 fields are correct, else 0.0
The final challenge score is the mean row score across all test listings.

Field Weights
Field weights are derived from the training label distribution. For each field, the weight is proportional to the minority fraction (1 − P_majority) — fields whose dominant value is very common (e.g. condition=new at 89.5% of train) receive near-zero weight, while fields with diverse value distributions (e.g. brand, variant) receive the highest weight. This means correctly predicting a rare brand or unusual variant contributes far more to the score than always predicting condition=new.

Field	Majority class	P_majority	Weight
brand	luma	0.064	0.1651
product_type	tote bag	0.105	0.1580
variant	mini	0.071	0.1639
color	clear	0.114	0.1564
size	na	0.305	0.1227
pack_count	1	0.617	0.0676
material	plastic	0.163	0.1477
condition	new	0.895	0.0186
A model that always predicts the majority class for every field scores 0.176, compared to 0.292 under equal field weights.

The exact-row-match component (30% of the score) rewards models that get every attribute right simultaneously, not just approximately correct on the easy fields.

Higher is better. A perfect score of 1.0 means every field matches exactly on every test listing.

Dataset
The prepared dataset contains a strict chronological split of marketplace listing drafts:

public/train.csv — 23,813 labeled listings from 2024-01-02 08:00:00 through 2024-05-24 19:59:00

extra_files.solution.py:
"""
Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization
T5-large Seq2Seq + Gradient Checkpointing (L4-safe)
"""
import os, time
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import torch
from collections import Counter
from difflib import get_close_matches
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
from torch.optim import AdamW
from transformers import T5Tokenizer, T5ForConditionalGeneration, get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

DATA_DIR = "./dataset/public"
OUTPUT_DIR = "./working"
MODEL_NAME = "t5-large"
MAX_INPUT_LEN = 320
MAX_TARGET_LEN = 128
BATCH_SIZE = 8          # Small batch to avoid OOM
GRAD_ACCUM = 4          # Effective batch = 32
NUM_EPOCHS = 6
LR = 1e-4               # Lower LR for large model
SEED = 42

FIELDS = ["brand","product_type","variant","color","size","pack_count","material","condition"]
FIELD_WEIGHTS = {"brand":0.1651,"product_type":0.1580,"variant":0.1639,"color":0.1564,
                 "size":0.1227,"pack_count":0.0676,"material":0.1477,"condition":0.0186}
TASK_PREFIX = "canonicalize: "

def parse_spec(s):
    d = {}
    if pd.isna(s): return {f:"na" for f in FIELDS}
    for p in s.split(";"): 
        p=p.strip()
        if "=" in p: k,v=p.split("=",1); d[k.strip()]=v.strip()
    return d

def format_spec(d):
    return "; ".join(f"{f}={d.get(f,'na')}" for f in FIELDS)

def build_input_text(row):
    parts = [f"title: {str(row.get('raw_title','')).strip()}",
             f"bullets: {str(row.get('raw_bullets','')).strip()}",
             f"description: {str(row.get('raw_description','')).strip()}",
             f"region: {str(row.get('marketplace_region','')).strip()}",
             f"locale: {str(row.get('locale','')).strip()}",
             f"seller_tier: {str(row.get('seller_tier','')).strip()}",
             f"taxonomy: {str(row.get('taxonomy_hint','')).strip()}",
             f"price: {str(row.get('price_local','')).strip()}"]
    for h in ["brand_hint","color_hint","size_hint","pack_count_hint","material_hint"]:
        v = row.get(h,"")
        if pd.notna(v) and str(v).strip().lower() not in ("","nan"):
            parts.append(f"hint_{h.replace('_hint','')}: {str(v).strip()}")
    return " | ".join(parts)

def clamp(val, field, vocabs):
    val = val.strip().lower()
    if val in vocabs[field]: return val
    m = get_close_matches(val, vocabs[field], n=1, cutoff=0.6)
    return m[0] if m else val

def postprocess(pred_str, vocabs):
    parsed = parse_spec(pred_str)
    return format_spec({f: clamp(parsed.get(f,"na"), f, vocabs) for f in FIELDS})

class ListingDS(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, inputs, targets=None):
        self.inputs = inputs; self.targets = targets
    def __len__(self): return len(self.inputs)
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        item = {"input_text": TASK_PREFIX + self.inputs[idx]}
        if self.targets: item["target_text"] = self.targets[idx]
        return item

def main():
    torch.manual_seed(SEED); np.random.seed(SEED)
    os.makedirs(OUTPUT_DIR, exist_ok=True)
    device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    bf16 = torch.cuda.is_available() and torch.cuda.is_bf16_supported()

    train_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "train.csv"))
    test_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "test.csv"))

    field_vocabs = {}
    for f in FIELDS:
        vals = train_df["canonical_listing_spec"].apply(lambda x: parse_spec(x).get(f,"na"))
        field_vocabs[f] = sorted(vals.unique().tolist())

    train_inputs = train_df.apply(build_input_text, axis=1).tolist()
    train_targets = train_df["canonical_listing_spec"].tolist()
    test_inputs = test_df.apply(build_input_text, axis=1).tolist()

    tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME, legacy=False)

    def collate_fn(batch):
        enc = tokenizer([b["input_text"] for b in batch], max_length=MAX_INPUT_LEN,
                        padding="longest", truncation=True, return_tensors="pt")
        r = {"input_ids": enc["input_ids"], "attention_mask": enc["attention_mask"]}
        if "target_text" in batch[0]:
            tgt = tokenizer([b["target_text"] for b in batch], max_length=MAX_TARGET_LEN,
                            padding="longest", truncation=True, return_tensors="pt")
            labels = tgt["input_ids"]; labels[labels==tokenizer.pad_token_id] = -100
            r["labels"] = labels
        return r

    # Split
    ptypes = train_df["canonical_listing_spec"].apply(lambda x: parse_spec(x).get("product_type","na"))
    train_idx, val_idx = train_test_split(range(len(train_inputs)), test_size=0.08,
                                           random_state=SEED, stratify=ptypes)

    train_ds = ListingDS([train_inputs[i] for i in train_idx], [train_targets[i] for i in train_idx])
    val_ds = ListingDS([train_inputs[i] for i in val_idx], [train_targets[i] for i in val_idx])
    test_ds = ListingDS(test_inputs)

    train_loader = DataLoader(train_ds, batch_size=BATCH_SIZE, shuffle=True, num_workers=2,
                              pin_memory=True, drop_last=True, collate_fn=collate_fn)
    val_loader = DataLoader(val_ds, batch_size=16, shuffle=False, num_workers=2,
                            pin_memory=True, collate_fn=collate_fn)
    test_loader = DataLoader(test_ds, batch_size=16, shuffle=False, num_workers=2,
                             pin_memory=True, collate_fn=collate_fn)

    # --- Model with gradient checkpointing ---
    model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
    model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()  # Save ~40% VRAM
    if bf16: model = model.to(torch.bfloat16)
    model.to(device)

    # Print memory usage
    if torch.cuda.is_available():
        mem = torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9
        print(f"Model loaded: {mem:.1f} GB VRAM")

    optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=LR, weight_decay=0.01, eps=1e-6)
    total_steps = (len(train_loader) // GRAD_ACCUM) * NUM_EPOCHS
    warmup_steps = int(0.08 * total_steps)
    scheduler = get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup(optimizer, warmup_steps, total_steps)

    print(f"Steps/epoch: {len(train_loader)}, Effective batch: {BATCH_SIZE*GRAD_ACCUM}")
    print(f"Total optimizer steps: {total_steps}")

    best_score = 0.0
    best_path = os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, "best_model.pt")

    for epoch in range(NUM_EPOCHS):
        model.train()
        epoch_loss = 0.0
        t0 = time.time()
        optimizer.zero_grad()

        for step, batch in enumerate(train_loader):
            ids = batch["input_ids"].to(device)
            mask = batch["attention_mask"].to(device)
            labels = batch["labels"].to(device)

            with torch.amp.autocast("cuda", dtype=torch.bfloat16, enabled=bf16):
                loss = model(input_ids=ids, attention_mask=mask, labels=labels).loss
                loss = loss / GRAD_ACCUM

            loss.backward()
            epoch_loss += loss.item() * GRAD_ACCUM

            if (step + 1) % GRAD_ACCUM == 0:
                torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
                optimizer.step()
                optimizer.zero_grad()
                scheduler.step()

            if (step + 1) % 200 == 0:
                print(f"  Epoch {epoch+1} Step {step+1}/{len(train_loader)} "
                      f"Loss={epoch_loss/(step+1):.4f} Time={time.time()-t0:.0f}s")

        # Validation (no grad checkpointing needed for eval)
        model.eval()
        val_preds = []
        val_truths = [train_targets[i] for i in val_idx]

        with torch.no_grad():
            for batch in val_loader:
                gen = model.generate(input_ids=batch["input_ids"].to(device),
                                     attention_mask=batch["attention_mask"].to(device),
                                     max_length=MAX_TARGET_LEN, num_beams=4,
                                     early_stopping=True)
                val_preds.extend(tokenizer.batch_decode(gen, skip_special_tokens=True))

        # Score
        scores = []; exact = 0; fc = {f:0 for f in FIELDS}
        for p, t in zip(val_preds, val_truths):
            pd_ = parse_spec(p); td = parse_spec(t)
            wp = 0; ok = True
            for f in FIELDS:
                pv = clamp(pd_.get(f,"na"), f, field_vocabs)
                tv = td.get(f,"na").strip().lower()
                if pv == tv: wp += FIELD_WEIGHTS[f]; fc[f] += 1
                else: ok = False
            if ok: exact += 1
            scores.append(0.70*wp + 0.30*(1 if ok else 0))
        vs = np.mean(scores)

        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1}/{NUM_EPOCHS} | Loss={epoch_loss/len(train_loader):.4f} | "
              f"RWSAA={vs:.4f} | Exact={exact}/{len(val_truths)} | Time={time.time()-t0:.0f}s")
        for f in FIELDS:
            print(f"  {f}: {fc[f]}/{len(val_truths)} ({100*fc[f]/len(val_truths):.1f}%)")

        if vs > best_score:
            best_score = vs
            torch.save(model.state_dict(), best_path)
            print(f"  Best saved: {vs:.4f}")

        # Clear cache between epochs
        torch.cuda.empty_cache()

    # Reload best
    model.load_state_dict(torch.load(best_path, map_location=device))
    model.eval()

    # Inference
    print("Generating test predictions...")
    all_preds = []
    with torch.no_grad():
        for batch in test_loader:
            gen = model.generate(input_ids=batch["input_ids"].to(device),
                                 attention_mask=batch["attention_mask"].to(device),
                                 max_length=MAX_TARGET_LEN, num_beams=5,
                                 early_stopping=True, length_penalty=1.0)
            all_preds.extend(tokenizer.batch_decode(gen, skip_special_tokens=True))

    processed = [postprocess(p, field_vocabs) for p in all_preds]

    sub = pd.DataFrame({"listing_id": test_df["listing_id"], "canonical_listing_spec": processed})
    sub.to_csv(os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, "submission.csv"), index=False)
    print(f"Saved submission.csv")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
extra_files.solver_context.md:
# Solver Context

You are working on the challenge `Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization`.

## Goal
Build a real Machine Learning solution that can generalize to unseen
data. Focus on clean, reusable code and a notebook that can run on
Google Colab with A100 hardware.

## Required outputs
- Main notebook: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization/notebook.ipynb`
- Working directory for experiments: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization/working`
- Dataset directory: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization/dataset`
- Repacked Colab archive: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Marketplace Listing Attribute Canonicalization/dataset.zip`

## Challenge description
Overview
Marketplace catalogs depend on clean, normalized product records for search ranking, deduplication, moderation, and downstream quality systems. In practice, sellers submit noisy drafts with inconsistent casing, shorthand attributes, locale-specific size formats, keyword stuffing, and partially missing structured hints. In this challenge, your task is to generate the canonical marketplace attribute string for each draft listing.

Each row is one seller-created listing draft. The dataset provides the raw seller text (raw_title, raw_bullets, raw_description), marketplace context, and noisy extracted hints. Your output must be a single normalized string in an exact key order, containing 8 canonical attributes: brand, product type, variant, color, size, pack count, material, and condition.

This is a sequence-to-sequence normalization benchmark. Strong solutions should read the raw listing text directly, normalize abbreviations and locale-specific forms, use metadata carefully, and enforce the exact target format. Weak solutions usually trust the hint columns too much or produce free-form text that does not match the required canonical schema.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored using Rarity-Weighted Structured Attribute Accuracy.

For each listing:

Parse the predicted canonical_listing_spec into 8 required key-value fields.
Parse the ground-truth canonical_listing_spec into the same 8 fields.
Compute a weighted partial score and an exact-row indicator, then blend:
row_score = 0.70 × weighted_partial + 0.30 × exact_row_match  
Where:

weighted_partial = Σ FIELD_WEIGHTS[k] × (pred_k == truth_k) across all 8 fields; weights sum to 1.0
exact_row_match = 1.0 if all 8 fields are correct, else 0.0
The final challenge score is the mean row score across all test listings.

Field Weights
Field weights are derived from the training label distribution. For each field, the weight is proportional to the minority fraction (1 − P_majority) — fields whose dominant value is very common (e.g. condition=new at 89.5% of train) receive near-zero weight, while fields with diverse value distributions (e.g. brand, variant) receive the highest weight. This means correctly predicting a rare brand or unusual variant contributes far more to the score than always predicting condition=new.

Field	Majority class	P_majority	Weight
brand	luma	0.064	0.1651
product_type	tote bag	0.105	0.1580
variant	mini	0.071	0.1639
color	clear	0.114	0.1564
size	na	0.305	0.1227
pack_count	1	0.617	0.0676
material	plastic	0.163	0.1477
condition	new	0.895	0.0186
A model that always predicts the majority class for every field scores 0.176, compared to 0.292 under equal field weights.

The exact-row-match component (30% of the score) rewards models that get every attribute right simultaneously, not just approximately correct on the easy fields.

Higher is better. A perfect score of 1.0 means every field matches exactly on every test listing.

Dataset
The prepared dataset contains a strict chronological split of marketplace listing drafts:

public/train.csv — 23,813 labeled listings from 2024-01-02 08:00:00 through 2024-05-24 19:59:00
public/test.csv — 6,187 unlabeled listings from 2024-05-25 08:00:00 through 2024-06-30 19:59:00
public/sample_submission.csv — example submission showing the required output schema
private/answers.csv — hidden ground-truth canonical strings for scoring
Important dataset characteristics:

each row is one listing draft; there is no candidate expansion or grouping
train.csv contains 16 feature columns plus the target column canonical_listing_spec
test.csv contains the same 16 feature columns without the target
the main modeling signal is in raw_title, raw_bullets, and raw_description
the hint fields brand_hint, color_hint, size_hint, pack_count_hint, and material_hint are frequently missing and are actively wrong in roughly 22-32% of non-empty cases; they cannot be trusted without corroboration from the raw text
taxonomy_hint is often broadened or replaced with an entirely different product category; it is a weak signal at best
approximately 18% of rows per field have all mentions suppressed from text and the matching hint set to blank; the model must fall back to priors or cross-field reasoning for those rows
variant descriptions use a paraphrase vocabulary that partially shifts in the test period; some test-period phrasings do not appear in any training row
the target always follows the same fixed key order:
brand=<...>; product_type=<...>; variant=<...>; color=<...>; size=<...>; pack_count=<...>; material=<...>; condition=<...>
The prepared files contain these columns:

Identifiers and Marketplace Context
Column	Type	Description
listing_id	string	Unique listing identifier used in submissions
created_at	datetime	Listing creation timestamp
seller_id	string	Seller identifier
seller_tier	categorical	Seller maturity tier: new, growth, established, or premium
marketplace_region	categorical	Marketplace operating region
locale	categorical	Seller locale
taxonomy_hint	string	Noisy or coarse taxonomy path hint
price_local	float	Seller-entered local price
Raw Listing Text
Column	Type	Description
raw_title	string	Seller-created title draft with noise and shorthand
raw_bullets	string	Pipe-separated bullet-style seller notes
raw_description	string	Longer free-form draft description
Noisy Extracted Hints
Column	Type	Description
brand_hint	string	Noisy extracted brand hint, sometimes stale or missing
color_hint	string	Noisy extracted color hint, sometimes missing
size_hint	string	Noisy extracted size or compatibility hint, sometimes missing
pack_count_hint	string	Noisy extracted pack-count hint, sometimes missing
material_hint	string	Noisy extracted material hint, sometimes missing
Target
Column	Type	Description

Submission
Submit a submission.csv file with the following format:

Column	Type	Description
listing_id	string	Listing identifier copied from test.csv
canonical_listing_spec	string	Predicted canonical attribute string in the exact required schema
Example:

listing_id,canonical_listing_spec

lst_023860,"brand=novara; product_type=phone case; variant=magnetic; color=black; size=iphone 15; pack_count=2; material=silicone; condition=new"  
 

Expected Output

mathematical graph property classificati.name: Mathematical Graph Property Classification
mathematical graph property classificati.description:
Overview
This is a computer-vision-focused graph understanding task. Each example is a rendered PNG plot (train is grayscale; test may include slight nuisance rendering changes). The model should use the image to predict three categorical targets:

function_family: one of 12 function families
integral_sign: integral_sign: whether the signed integral over the shown x-interval is negative, zero, or positive.
x_intercept_count: the number of discrete x-axis intercepts visible in the plotted interval
The image contains no formula text. The region between the plotted graph and the x-axis is shaded across the visible interval.

The challenge is designed to test whether a model can extract mathematical meaning directly from a plotted graph, rather than from symbolic expressions or textual annotations. To solve it well, a model must read visual properties such as curve shape, oscillation pattern, discontinuities, intercept behavior, and the sign of the shaded area, and map them to structured categorical outputs.

More broadly, the benchmark evaluates visual mathematical reasoning: not just recognizing that an image is a graph, but inferring function-level properties from it. The three targets are intended to capture complementary skills: identifying the overall function type, reasoning about the signed area over the visible interval, and counting visible x-axis crossings under the task definition.

The challenge also tests generalization beyond simple memorization. Train and test examples follow different parameter regimes, and test formulas are held out from the training split by exact formula. As a result, strong performance requires learning graph semantics and visual-function relationships rather than matching previously seen formulas.

Overall, this task aims to measure how well computer vision models can connect visual graph structure to mathematical concepts in a clean synthetic setting.

Public Files

public/

  train.csv

  test.csv

  sample_submission.csv

  images/

    train/

      train_00000.png ... train_00999.png

    test/

      test_00000.png ... test_00999.png

Columns
train.csv has 1,000 rows.

| column | type | meaning |

|---|---:|---|

| id | string | Unique training example ID. |

| image_path | string | Relative path from public/ to the PNG plot. |

| function_family | string | One of the 12 family labels. |

| integral_sign | string | One of negative, zero, positive. |

| x_intercept_count | string | One of 0, 1, 2, 3_or_more. |

test.csv has 1,000 rows and contains only id and image_path.

Labels
Allowed function_family labels:


linear

quadratic

cubic

exponential

logarithmic

sine

cosine

tangent

sinc

heaviside_step

rectangular_pulse

dirac_impulse

Allowed integral_sign labels:


negative

zero

positive

Allowed x_intercept_count labels:


0

1

2

3_or_more

For step and rectangular-pulse plots, flat segments that lie on the x-axis are not counted as discrete x-intercepts.

Submission Format
Submit a CSV with exactly these columns:


id,function_family,integral_sign,x_intercept_count

test_00000,linear,zero,1

test_00001,quadratic,positive,0

Evaluation
The score is the weighted mean of the three target accuracies:


score = (
    0.6 * accuracy(function_family)
    + 0.2 * accuracy(integral_sign)
    + 0.2 * accuracy(x_intercept_count)
)

The score is a float from 0.0 to 1.0.

Constraints
Training rows: 1,000.
Test rows: 1,000.
Training plots use x from -2.5 to 2.5 and y from -5 to 5. Test plots use held-out axis ranges.
Test functions are held out from the training split by exact formula.
Test formulas use different parameter regimes from train formulas.
Training plots are grayscale. Test plots may include slight nuisance changes.
Tangent discontinuities are shown as separated curve segments.
Dirac impulse plots are rendered as vertical spikes.
 

What Not To Use
No LLM or VLM models (no pretrained language models)
No Ocr etc
No rule based solutions or components, only allowed PNG in prediction out.

extra_files.solution.py:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Mathematical Graph Property Classification — Optimized Solution
================================================================
Multi-head dual-backbone CNN ensemble for classifying mathematical function plots.

Key techniques:
1. Plot area extraction — removes axis labels causing train→test domain shift
2. Grayscale normalization — eliminates color rendering differences
3. Grid line removal — cleans tick marks and grid within plot area
4. Dual backbone ensemble (ConvNeXt-Tiny + EfficientNetV2-S)
5. Heavy domain-shift augmentation (ElasticTransform, GridDistortion, wide crop)
6. SWA (Stochastic Weight Averaging) for generalization
7. 5-fold CV + 8-way TTA for robust inference

Input:  ./dataset/public/
Output: ./working/submission.csv
"""

import os
import sys
import re
import gc
import time
import random
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import cv2

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
from torch.optim import AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import CosineAnnealingWarmRestarts
from torch.optim.swa_utils import AveragedModel, SWALR, update_bn

import timm
import albumentations as A
from albumentations.pytorch import ToTensorV2
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold
from collections import Counter


# ============================================================
# 1. Configuration
# ============================================================
class CFG:
    seed = 42
    img_size = 288
    backbones = [
        'convnext_tiny.in12k_ft_in1k',         # Best CV: 0.9382
        'tf_efficientnetv2_s.in21k_ft_in1k',   # Diversity: 0.9260
    ]
    n_folds = 5
    epochs = 50
    swa_start = 30
    batch_size = 32
    lr = 2e-4
    weight_decay = 5e-4
    label_smoothing = 0.03
    mixup_alpha = 0.4
    mixup_prob = 0.5
    tta_count = 8
    num_workers = 2
    patience = 12
    device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
    # Labels
    family_labels = [
        'linear', 'quadratic', 'cubic', 'exponential', 'logarithmic',
        'sine', 'cosine', 'tangent', 'sinc', 'heaviside_step',
        'rectangular_pulse', 'dirac_impulse',
    ]
    integral_labels = ['negative', 'zero', 'positive']
    intercept_labels = ['0', '1', '2', '3_or_more']


def seed_everything(seed):
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False


# ============================================================
# 2. Data paths
# ============================================================
def find_data_root(base_paths):
    """Find the actual data root containing train.csv."""
    for path in base_paths:
        if os.path.exists(os.path.join(path, 'train.csv')):
            return path
        # Check subdirectories
        if os.path.isdir(path):
            for d in os.listdir(path):
                fp = os.path.join(path, d)
                if os.path.isdir(fp) and os.path.exists(os.path.join(fp, 'train.csv')):
                    return fp
    raise FileNotFoundError(f'Could not find train.csv in: {base_paths}')


# ============================================================
# 3. Preprocessing — THE CRITICAL FIX for domain shift
# ============================================================
def detect_plot_frame(img_gray):
    """
    Detect the plot frame boundary using morphological line detection.
    
    Train images have axis labels at x∈[-2.5,2.5], y∈[-5,5]
    Test images have DIFFERENT axis ranges and teal/green color.
    The model must NOT see these labels — only the curve and shading.
    
    Returns (top, bottom, left, right) pixel coordinates.
    """
    h, w = img_gray.shape

    # Threshold to find dark elements
    _, binary_inv = cv2.threshold(img_gray, 180, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY_INV)

    # Detect horizontal frame lines
    kernel_h = np.ones((1, w // 3), np.uint8)
    h_lines = cv2.morphologyEx(binary_inv, cv2.MORPH_OPEN, kernel_h)
    h_proj = np.sum(h_lines > 0, axis=1)
    h_peaks = np.where(h_proj > w * 0.2)[0]

    # Detect vertical frame lines
    kernel_v = np.ones((h // 3, 1), np.uint8)
    v_lines = cv2.morphologyEx(binary_inv, cv2.MORPH_OPEN, kernel_v)
    v_proj = np.sum(v_lines > 0, axis=0)
    v_peaks = np.where(v_proj > h * 0.2)[0]

    if len(h_peaks) >= 2 and len(v_peaks) >= 2:
        top, bottom = h_peaks[0], h_peaks[-1]
        left, right = v_peaks[0], v_peaks[-1]
    else:
        # Fallback: typical matplotlib margins
        top = int(h * 0.07)
        bottom = int(h * 0.83)
        left = int(w * 0.13)
        right = int(w * 0.95)

    # Sanity check: ensure reasonable crop
    if (bottom - top) < h * 0.3 or (right - left) < w * 0.3:
        top = int(h * 0.07)
        bottom = int(h * 0.83)
        left = int(w * 0.13)
        right = int(w * 0.95)

    return top, bottom, left, right


def remove_grid_lines(img_gray):
    """
    Remove thin grid lines and tick marks from the plot area.
    These are scale-dependent artifacts that differ between train and test.
    Only removes lines that span >40% of the dimension (true grid lines).
    Preserves the actual curve which is thicker and doesn't span full width/height.
    """
    h, w = img_gray.shape
    result = img_gray.copy()

    # Invert for morphology (dark -> white)
    inv = cv2.bitwise_not(img_gray)

    # Detect long horizontal lines (grid + axis lines inside frame)
    kw = max(w // 3, 20)
    kernel_h = np.ones((1, kw), np.uint8)
    h_lines = cv2.morphologyEx(inv, cv2.MORPH_OPEN, kernel_h)

    # Detect long vertical lines
    kh = max(h // 3, 20)
    kernel_v = np.ones((kh, 1), np.uint8)
    v_lines = cv2.morphologyEx(inv, cv2.MORPH_OPEN, kernel_v)

    # Only remove thin lines (dilate by 1-2px to cover anti-aliasing)
    thin_mask = cv2.dilate(h_lines + v_lines, np.ones((2, 2), np.uint8))

    # Set thin grid lines to white (background)
    result[thin_mask > 0] = 255

    return result


def preprocess_image(img_bgr):
    """
    Full preprocessing pipeline:
    1. Convert to grayscale → eliminates train(gray) vs test(teal) color shift
    2. Detect and crop plot area → removes axis labels causing domain shift
    3. Remove grid lines → eliminates scale-dependent grid artifacts
    4. Apply CLAHE → enhance curve contrast
    5. Replicate to 3 channels → for ImageNet-pretrained backbone
    """
    # Step 1: Grayscale
    gray = cv2.cvtColor(img_bgr, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)

    # Step 2: Detect and crop plot frame
    top, bottom, left, right = detect_plot_frame(gray)
    pad = 3
    t = min(top + pad, bottom - 1)
    b = max(bottom - pad, top + 1)
    l = min(left + pad, right - 1)
    r = max(right - pad, left + 1)
    cropped = gray[t:b, l:r]

    if cropped.shape[0] < 20 or cropped.shape[1] < 20:
        cropped = gray

    # Step 3: Remove grid lines and ticks
    cleaned = remove_grid_lines(cropped)

    # Step 4: CLAHE for contrast enhancement
    clahe = cv2.createCLAHE(clipLimit=2.0, tileGridSize=(8, 8))
    enhanced = clahe.apply(cleaned)

    # Step 5: Replicate to 3 channels
    rgb = cv2.cvtColor(enhanced, cv2.COLOR_GRAY2RGB)

    return rgb


# ============================================================
# 4. Augmentations
# ============================================================
def get_train_transforms(img_size):
    """Heavy augmentation to handle remaining domain shift after preprocessing."""
    return A.Compose([
        A.RandomResizedCrop(
            size=(img_size, img_size),
            scale=(0.6, 1.0), ratio=(0.85, 1.15), p=1.0
        ),
        A.ShiftScaleRotate(
            shift_limit=0.08, scale_limit=0.15, rotate_limit=5,
            border_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, value=255, p=0.5
        ),
        A.ElasticTransform(
            alpha=30, sigma=5,
            border_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, value=255, p=0.2
        ),
        A.GridDistortion(
            num_steps=5, distort_limit=0.15,
            border_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, value=255, p=0.2
        ),
        A.Perspective(
            scale=(0.02, 0.06), pad_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, pad_val=255, p=0.2
        ),
        A.OneOf([
            A.GaussNoise(var_limit=(10, 60), p=1.0),
            A.GaussianBlur(blur_limit=(3, 5), p=1.0),
            A.MedianBlur(blur_limit=3, p=1.0),
            A.Sharpen(alpha=(0.1, 0.3), lightness=(0.9, 1.1), p=1.0),
        ], p=0.4),
        A.OneOf([
            A.RandomBrightnessContrast(brightness_limit=0.15, contrast_limit=0.2, p=1.0),
            A.CLAHE(clip_limit=2.0, p=1.0),
            A.RandomGamma(gamma_limit=(80, 120), p=1.0),
        ], p=0.4),
        A.CoarseDropout(
            max_holes=6, max_height=img_size // 10, max_width=img_size // 10,
            min_holes=1, min_height=img_size // 25, min_width=img_size // 25,
            fill_value=255, p=0.3
        ),
        A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
        ToTensorV2(),
    ])


def get_valid_transforms(img_size):
    return A.Compose([
        A.Resize(img_size, img_size),
        A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
        ToTensorV2(),
    ])


def get_tta_transforms(img_size):
    """8 TTA variants for robust inference."""
    return [
        get_valid_transforms(img_size),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size + 32, img_size + 32),
            A.CenterCrop(img_size, img_size),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size - 24, img_size - 24),
            A.PadIfNeeded(img_size, img_size, border_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, value=255),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size, img_size),
            A.Rotate(limit=(3, 3), border_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, value=255, p=1.0),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size, img_size),
            A.Rotate(limit=(-3, -3), border_mode=cv2.BORDER_CONSTANT, value=255, p=1.0),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size, img_size),
            A.RandomBrightnessContrast(brightness_limit=0.0, contrast_limit=(0.12, 0.12), p=1.0),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size, img_size),
            A.CLAHE(clip_limit=2.0, p=1.0),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
        A.Compose([
            A.Resize(img_size + 56, img_size + 56),
            A.CenterCrop(img_size, img_size),
            A.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
            ToTensorV2(),
        ]),
    ]


# ============================================================
# 5. Dataset
# ============================================================
class GraphDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, df, image_cache, transform=None, is_test=False):
        self.df = df.reset_index(drop=True)
        self.image_cache = image_cache
        self.transform = transform
        self.is_test = is_test

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.df)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        row = self.df.iloc[idx]
        img = self.image_cache[row['id']].copy()

        if self.transform:
            img = self.transform(image=img)['image']

        if self.is_test:
            return img
        return img, (
            torch.tensor(row['family_idx'], dtype=torch.long),
            torch.tensor(row['integral_idx'], dtype=torch.long),
            torch.tensor(row['intercept_idx'], dtype=torch.long),
        )


# ============================================================
# 6. Model
# ============================================================
class MultiHeadGraphClassifier(nn.Module):
    """Flexible backbone + 3 classification heads."""

    def __init__(self, backbone_name, n_family=12, n_integral=3, n_intercept=4,
                 pretrained=True, drop_rate=0.3):
        super().__init__()
        self.backbone = timm.create_model(
            backbone_name, pretrained=pretrained, num_classes=0, drop_rate=drop_rate,
        )
        d = self.backbone.num_features

        self.shared_bn = nn.BatchNorm1d(d)
        self.shared_drop = nn.Dropout(0.25)

        self.head_family = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d, 512), nn.BatchNorm1d(512), nn.SiLU(True), nn.Dropout(0.4),
            nn.Linear(512, 256), nn.BatchNorm1d(256), nn.SiLU(True), nn.Dropout(0.25),
            nn.Linear(256, n_family),
        )
        self.head_integral = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d, 256), nn.BatchNorm1d(256), nn.SiLU(True), nn.Dropout(0.35),
            nn.Linear(256, n_integral),
        )
        self.head_intercept = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d, 256), nn.BatchNorm1d(256), nn.SiLU(True), nn.Dropout(0.35),
            nn.Linear(256, n_intercept),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        feat = self.shared_drop(self.shared_bn(self.backbone(x)))
        return self.head_family(feat), self.head_integral(feat), self.head_intercept(feat)


# ============================================================
# 7. Loss
# ============================================================
class FocalLoss(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, gamma=2.0, label_smoothing=0.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.ls = label_smoothing

    def forward(self, logits, targets):
        ce = F.cross_entropy(logits, targets, reduction='none', label_smoothing=self.ls)
        focal = ((1 - torch.exp(-ce)) ** self.gamma) * ce
        return focal.mean()


class MultiTaskLoss(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, gamma=2.0, label_smoothing=0.03):
        super().__init__()
        self.lf = FocalLoss(gamma, label_smoothing)
        self.li = FocalLoss(gamma, label_smoothing)
        self.lx = FocalLoss(gamma, label_smoothing)

    def forward(self, preds, targets):
        lf = self.lf(preds[0], targets[0])
        li = self.li(preds[1], targets[1])
        lx = self.lx(preds[2], targets[2])
        return 0.6 * lf + 0.2 * li + 0.2 * lx, (lf.item(), li.item(), lx.item())


# ============================================================
# 8. Mixup
# ============================================================
def mixup_data(x, targets, alpha=0.4):
    lam = max(np.random.beta(alpha, alpha), 0.5) if alpha > 0 else 1.0
    idx = torch.randperm(x.size(0), device=x.device)
    return lam * x + (1 - lam) * x[idx], targets, tuple(t[idx] for t in targets), lam


def mixup_criterion(criterion, preds, ta, tb, lam):
    la, da = criterion(preds, ta)
    lb, db = criterion(preds, tb)
    return lam * la + (1 - lam) * lb, tuple(lam * a + (1 - lam) * b for a, b in zip(da, db))


# ============================================================
# 9. Training
# ============================================================
def train_one_epoch(model, loader, criterion, optimizer, scheduler, epoch, cfg,
                    swa_model=None, swa_sched=None):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0
    preds_list = [[], [], []]
    true_list = [[], [], []]
    scaler = torch.amp.GradScaler('cuda', enabled=(cfg.device == 'cuda'))
    use_swa = swa_model is not None and epoch >= cfg.swa_start

    for bi, (images, targets) in enumerate(loader):
        images = images.to(cfg.device)
        tgt = tuple(t.to(cfg.device) for t in targets)

        do_mix = np.random.random() < cfg.mixup_prob and not use_swa
        if do_mix:
            images, ta, tb, lam = mixup_data(images, tgt, cfg.mixup_alpha)

        optimizer.zero_grad()
        with torch.amp.autocast('cuda', enabled=(cfg.device == 'cuda')):
            out = model(images)
            loss = mixup_criterion(criterion, out, ta, tb, lam)[0] if do_mix else criterion(out, tgt)[0]

        scaler.scale(loss).backward()
        scaler.unscale_(optimizer)
        nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
        scaler.step(optimizer)
        scaler.update()

        if use_swa:
            swa_model.update_parameters(model)
            if swa_sched:
                swa_sched.step()
        elif scheduler:
            scheduler.step(epoch + bi / len(loader))

        total_loss += loss.item()

        if not do_mix:
            for i in range(3):
                preds_list[i].append(out[i].argmax(1).cpu())
                true_list[i].append(tgt[i].cpu())

    avg_loss = total_loss / len(loader)
    if preds_list[0]:
        accs = [(torch.cat(preds_list[i]) == torch.cat(true_list[i])).float().mean().item() for i in range(3)]
        score = 0.6 * accs[0] + 0.2 * accs[1] + 0.2 * accs[2]
    else:
        accs, score = [0, 0, 0], 0
    return avg_loss, score, accs


@torch.no_grad()
def validate(model, loader, criterion, cfg):
    model.eval()
    total_loss = 0
    preds_list = [[], [], []]
    true_list = [[], [], []]

    for images, targets in loader:
        images = images.to(cfg.device)
        tgt = tuple(t.to(cfg.device) for t in targets)
        with torch.amp.autocast('cuda', enabled=(cfg.device == 'cuda')):
            out = model(images)
            loss = criterion(out, tgt)[0]
        total_loss += loss.item()
        for i in range(3):
            preds_list[i].append(out[i].argmax(1).cpu())
            true_list[i].append(tgt[i].cpu())

    avg_loss = total_loss / len(loader)
    accs = [(torch.cat(preds_list[i]) == torch.cat(true_list[i])).float().mean().item() for i in range(3)]
    score = 0.6 * accs[0] + 0.2 * accs[1] + 0.2 * accs[2]
    return avg_loss, score, accs


# ============================================================
# 10. Inference with TTA
# ============================================================
@torch.no_grad()
def predict_tta(model, test_df, image_cache, cfg):
    model.eval()
    tta_list = get_tta_transforms(cfg.img_size)[:cfg.tta_count]
    all_probs = [[], [], []]

    for tta_t in tta_list:
        ds = GraphDataset(test_df, image_cache, tta_t, is_test=True)
        dl = DataLoader(ds, batch_size=cfg.batch_size * 2, shuffle=False,
                        num_workers=cfg.num_workers, pin_memory=True)
        batch_probs = [[], [], []]
        for images in dl:
            images = images.to(cfg.device)
            with torch.amp.autocast('cuda', enabled=(cfg.device == 'cuda')):
                out = model(images)
            for i in range(3):
                batch_probs[i].append(F.softmax(out[i], dim=1).cpu())
        for i in range(3):
            all_probs[i].append(torch.cat(batch_probs[i]))

    return tuple(torch.stack(all_probs[i]).mean(0) for i in range(3))


# ============================================================
# MAIN
# ============================================================
def main():
    seed_everything(CFG.seed)

    # Find data
    DATA_ROOT = find_data_root([
        './dataset/public',
        './dataset',
        'dataset/public',
        'dataset',
    ])
    print(f'Data root: {DATA_ROOT}')
    print(f'Device: {CFG.device}')
    if CFG.device == 'cuda':
        print(f'GPU: {torch.cuda.get_device_name()}')

    # Load CSVs
    train_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_ROOT, 'train.csv'))
    test_df = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_ROOT, 'test.csv'))

    family2idx = {l: i for i, l in enumerate(CFG.family_labels)}
    integral2idx = {l: i for i, l in enumerate(CFG.integral_labels)}
    intercept2idx = {l: i for i, l in enumerate(CFG.intercept_labels)}

    train_df['family_idx'] = train_df['function_family'].map(family2idx)
    train_df['integral_idx'] = train_df['integral_sign'].map(integral2idx)
    train_df['intercept_idx'] = train_df['x_intercept_count'].astype(str).map(intercept2idx)

    # Preprocess ALL images into memory
    print('Preprocessing images (crop plot area + grayscale + clean grid + CLAHE)...')
    image_cache = {}
    for df in [train_df, test_df]:
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            img = cv2.imread(os.path.join(DATA_ROOT, row['image_path']))
            image_cache[row['id']] = preprocess_image(img)

    print(f'Cached {len(image_cache)} images')

    # Working dir
    os.makedirs('./working', exist_ok=True)

    # ── Train all backbones ──
    skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=CFG.n_folds, shuffle=True, random_state=CFG.seed)
    all_cv = {}

    for bb_idx, backbone in enumerate(CFG.backbones):
        print(f'\n{"#"*70}')
        print(f'BACKBONE {bb_idx+1}/{len(CFG.backbones)}: {backbone}')
        print(f'{"#"*70}')

        fold_scores = []
        for fold, (tr_idx, va_idx) in enumerate(skf.split(train_df, train_df['family_idx'])):
            print(f'\n{"="*60}')
            print(f'FOLD {fold+1}/{CFG.n_folds}')
            print(f'{"="*60}')

            tr_fold = train_df.iloc[tr_idx].reset_index(drop=True)
            va_fold = train_df.iloc[va_idx].reset_index(drop=True)
            print(f'Train: {len(tr_fold)}, Val: {len(va_fold)}')

            tr_ds = GraphDataset(tr_fold, image_cache, get_train_transforms(CFG.img_size))
            va_ds = GraphDataset(va_fold, image_cache, get_valid_transforms(CFG.img_size))
            tr_dl = DataLoader(tr_ds, batch_size=CFG.batch_size, shuffle=True,
                               num_workers=CFG.num_workers, pin_memory=True, drop_last=True)
            va_dl = DataLoader(va_ds, batch_size=CFG.batch_size * 2, shuffle=False,
                               num_workers=CFG.num_workers, pin_memory=True)

            model = MultiHeadGraphClassifier(backbone, pretrained=True, drop_rate=0.3).to(CFG.device)
            swa_model = AveragedModel(model)
            criterion = MultiTaskLoss(gamma=2.0, label_smoothing=CFG.label_smoothing)

            bb_params = list(model.backbone.parameters())
            hd_params = (list(model.head_family.parameters()) +
                         list(model.head_integral.parameters()) +
                         list(model.head_intercept.parameters()) +
                         list(model.shared_bn.parameters()))

            optimizer = AdamW([
                {'params': bb_params, 'lr': CFG.lr * 0.1},
                {'params': hd_params, 'lr': CFG.lr},
            ], weight_decay=CFG.weight_decay)

            scheduler = CosineAnnealingWarmRestarts(optimizer, T_0=CFG.swa_start, eta_min=1e-7)
            swa_sched = SWALR(optimizer, swa_lr=5e-5, anneal_epochs=5)

            best_score = 0
            no_improve = 0

            for epoch in range(CFG.epochs):
                t0 = time.time()
                use_swa = epoch >= CFG.swa_start

                tr_loss, tr_score, tr_acc = train_one_epoch(
                    model, tr_dl, criterion, optimizer, scheduler, epoch, CFG,
                    swa_model if use_swa else None, swa_sched if use_swa else None,
                )

                if use_swa:
                    update_bn(tr_dl, swa_model, device=CFG.device)
                    va_loss, va_score, va_acc = validate(swa_model, va_dl, criterion, CFG)
                else:
                    va_loss, va_score, va_acc = validate(model, va_dl, criterion, CFG)

                elapsed = time.time() - t0
                swa_tag = ' [SWA]' if use_swa else ''

                if (epoch + 1) % 10 == 0 or epoch == 0 or va_score > best_score:
                    print(f'Epoch {epoch+1:3d}/{CFG.epochs}{swa_tag} | '
                          f'Val score={va_score:.4f} [F={va_acc[0]:.3f} I={va_acc[1]:.3f} X={va_acc[2]:.3f}] | '
                          f'{elapsed:.1f}s')

                if va_score > best_score:
                    best_score = va_score
                    no_improve = 0
                    save_model = swa_model.module if use_swa else model
                    torch.save(save_model.state_dict(), f'./working/model_{bb_idx}_f{fold}.pt')
                    print(f'  ★ New best: {best_score:.4f}')
                else:
                    no_improve += 1
                    if no_improve >= CFG.patience and epoch >= CFG.swa_start + 5:
                        print(f'  Early stop at epoch {epoch+1}')
                        break

            fold_scores.append(best_score)
            print(f'Fold {fold+1} best: {best_score:.4f}')

            del model, swa_model, optimizer, scheduler, swa_sched, tr_dl, va_dl
            gc.collect()
            if CFG.device == 'cuda':
                torch.cuda.empty_cache()

        all_cv[backbone] = fold_scores
        print(f'\n{backbone} CV: {np.mean(fold_scores):.4f} ± {np.std(fold_scores):.4f}')

    print(f'\n{"="*70}')
    print('FINAL CV RESULTS:')
    for bb, scores in all_cv.items():
        print(f'  {bb}: {np.mean(scores):.4f} ± {np.std(scores):.4f}  {[f"{s:.4f}" for s in scores]}')

    # ── Inference: all backbones × all folds × TTA ──
    print(f'\n{"="*70}')
    print('INFERENCE')
    ens = [[], [], []]

    for bb_idx, backbone in enumerate(CFG.backbones):
        print(f'\nBackbone: {backbone}')
        for fold in range(CFG.n_folds):
            path = f'./working/model_{bb_idx}_f{fold}.pt'
            if not os.path.exists(path):
                continue
            print(f'  Fold {fold+1}...')
            model = MultiHeadGraphClassifier(backbone, pretrained=False, drop_rate=0.0).to(CFG.device)
            model.load_state_dict(torch.load(path, map_location=CFG.device))
            probs = predict_tta(model, test_df, image_cache, CFG)
            for i in range(3):
                ens[i].append(probs[i])
            del model
            gc.collect()
            if CFG.device == 'cuda':
                torch.cuda.empty_cache()

    n_models = len(ens[0])
    print(f'\nEnsembling {n_models} models...')
    final = [torch.stack(ens[i]).mean(0) for i in range(3)]

    # Decode predictions
    idx2fam = {i: l for i, l in enumerate(CFG.family_labels)}
    idx2int = {i: l for i, l in enumerate(CFG.integral_labels)}
    idx2xic = {i: l for i, l in enumerate(CFG.intercept_labels)}

    pred_fam = [idx2fam[i.item()] for i in final[0].argmax(1)]
    pred_int = [idx2int[i.item()] for i in final[1].argmax(1)]
    pred_xic = [idx2xic[i.item()] for i in final[2].argmax(1)]

    # Confidence stats
    print(f'\nConfidence: Fam={final[0].max(1)[0].mean():.4f}, '
          f'Int={final[1].max(1)[0].mean():.4f}, '
          f'Xic={final[2].max(1)[0].mean():.4f}')
    print(f'Family dist: {dict(Counter(pred_fam).most_common())}')

    # Create submission
    sub = pd.DataFrame({
        'id': test_df['id'],
        'function_family': pred_fam,
        'integral_sign': pred_int,
        'x_intercept_count': pred_xic,
    })

    sub.to_csv('./working/submission.csv', index=False)
    print(f'\nSaved: ./working/submission.csv ({sub.shape})')
    print(sub.head(10))

    # Verify
    sample = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA_ROOT, 'sample_submission.csv'))
    assert list(sub.columns) == list(sample.columns), 'Column mismatch'
    assert len(sub) == len(sample), 'Row count mismatch'
    assert set(sub['function_family']).issubset(set(CFG.family_labels))
    assert set(sub['integral_sign']).issubset(set(CFG.integral_labels))
    assert set(sub['x_intercept_count'].astype(str)).issubset(set(CFG.intercept_labels))
    print('\n✅ Submission verified!')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
extra_files.solver_context.md:
# Solver Context

You are working on the challenge `Mathematical Graph Property Classification`.

## Goal
Build a real Machine Learning solution that can generalize to unseen
data. Focus on clean, reusable code and a notebook that can run on
Google Colab with A100 hardware.

## Required outputs
- Main notebook: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Mathematical Graph Property Classification/notebook.ipynb`
- Working directory for experiments: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Mathematical Graph Property Classification/working`
- Dataset directory: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Mathematical Graph Property Classification/dataset`
- Repacked Colab archive: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Mathematical Graph Property Classification/dataset.zip`

## Challenge description
Overview
This is a computer-vision-focused graph understanding task. Each example is a rendered PNG plot (train is grayscale; test may include slight nuisance rendering changes). The model should use the image to predict three categorical targets:

function_family: one of 12 function families
integral_sign: integral_sign: whether the signed integral over the shown x-interval is negative, zero, or positive.
x_intercept_count: the number of discrete x-axis intercepts visible in the plotted interval
The image contains no formula text. The region between the plotted graph and the x-axis is shaded across the visible interval.

The challenge is designed to test whether a model can extract mathematical meaning directly from a plotted graph, rather than from symbolic expressions or textual annotations. To solve it well, a model must read visual properties such as curve shape, oscillation pattern, discontinuities, intercept behavior, and the sign of the shaded area, and map them to structured categorical outputs.

More broadly, the benchmark evaluates visual mathematical reasoning: not just recognizing that an image is a graph, but inferring function-level properties from it. The three targets are intended to capture complementary skills: identifying the overall function type, reasoning about the signed area over the visible interval, and counting visible x-axis crossings under the task definition.

The challenge also tests generalization beyond simple memorization. Train and test examples follow different parameter regimes, and test formulas are held out from the training split by exact formula. As a result, strong performance requires learning graph semantics and visual-function relationships rather than matching previously seen formulas.

Overall, this task aims to measure how well computer vision models can connect visual graph structure to mathematical concepts in a clean synthetic setting.

Public Files

public/

  train.csv

  test.csv

  sample_submission.csv

  images/

    train/

      train_00000.png ... train_00999.png

    test/

      test_00000.png ... test_00999.png

Columns
train.csv has 1,000 rows.

| column | type | meaning |

|---|---:|---|

| id | string | Unique training example ID. |

| image_path | string | Relative path from public/ to the PNG plot. |

| function_family | string | One of the 12 family labels. |

| integral_sign | string | One of negative, zero, positive. |

| x_intercept_count | string | One of 0, 1, 2, 3_or_more. |

test.csv has 1,000 rows and contains only id and image_path.

Labels
Allowed function_family labels:


linear

quadratic

cubic

exponential

logarithmic

sine

cosine

tangent

sinc

heaviside_step

rectangular_pulse

dirac_impulse

Allowed integral_sign labels:


negative

zero

positive

Allowed x_intercept_count labels:


0

1

2

3_or_more

For step and rectangular-pulse plots, flat segments that lie on the x-axis are not counted as discrete x-intercepts.

Submission Format
Submit a CSV with exactly these columns:


id,function_family,integral_sign,x_intercept_count

test_00000,linear,zero,1

test_00001,quadratic,positive,0

Evaluation
The score is the weighted mean of the three target accuracies:


score = (
    0.6 * accuracy(function_family)
    + 0.2 * accuracy(integral_sign)
    + 0.2 * accuracy(x_intercept_count)
)

The score is a float from 0.0 to 1.0.

Constraints
Training rows: 1,000.
Test rows: 1,000.
Training plots use x from -2.5 to 2.5 and y from -5 to 5. Test plots use held-out axis ranges.
Test functions are held out from the training split by exact formula.
Test formulas use different parameter regimes from train formulas.
Training plots are grayscale. Test plots may include slight nuisance changes.
Tangent discontinuities are shown as separated curve segments.
Dirac impulse plots are rendered as vertical spikes.
 

What Not To Use
No LLM or VLM models (no pretrained language models)
No Ocr etc
No rule based solutions or components, only allowed PNG in prediction out.

## Solver policy

multi granularity legislative summarizat.name: Multi Granularity Legislative Summarization With Entity Preservation
multi granularity legislative summarizat.description:
Overview
Long-form statutory-register documents — formally drafted texts that establish rules, allocations, and responsibilities — require summaries that go beyond generic abstraction. A reader of such a document needs a plain-language description of what the document does, the specific monetary allocations it authorises, the effective dates it references, and which named bodies are tasked with implementation. Standard summarization benchmarks score only verbatim overlap with a reference (ROUGE) and ignore whether the summary faithfully preserves the specific factual content that makes a statutory summary actionable.

This challenge reframes long-document summarization as a multi-granularity, entity-faithful task. Given the full text of a formal statutory-style document, your system must produce two outputs at different granularities — a short title and a detailed plain-language summary — and is scored on how well both outputs preserve the specific verifiable entities appearing in the reference summary, in addition to overall textual and semantic overlap.

The source documents are pre-processed to remove header metadata that would identify specific instances, and document identifiers in the test split are opaque hashes with no correspondence to any public catalogue.

Task
For each test document, predict:

predicted_title — a short, self-contained title for the document (≤ 100 characters).
predicted_summary — a plain-language summary of the document's provisions (target length 50–400 words).
The predicted summary must preserve verifiable legal entities from the reference (monetary amounts, fiscal-year references, statutory section citations, and named agencies / acts) to score well on the entity-preservation component.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored using a composite multi-objective metric:

Score = 0.30 * Summary_ROUGE_L
      + 0.15 * Title_ROUGE_L
      + 0.30 * Entity_Preservation_F1
      + 0.15 * TFIDF_Cosine
      + 0.10 * Structure_Compliance
Summary ROUGE-L (weight 0.30) — Longest-common-subsequence F1 between your predicted_summary and the reference summary, computed on whitespace-tokenised lowercased text. Rewards verbatim and structural overlap with the reference.

Title ROUGE-L (weight 0.15) — Same metric applied to predicted_title versus the reference title. Multi-granularity output is a core novelty of this challenge; standard summarization benchmarks produce only one target.

Entity Preservation F1 (weight 0.30) — The primary novel component. Four categories of legal entities are extracted from the reference summary with deterministic regex rules:

monetary amounts (e.g. $100,000, $1.5 million)
fiscal-year references (e.g. fiscal year 2025, FY 2026)
statutory section citations (e.g. Section 203, Sec. 101(a))
named acts / agencies (Title-Case multi-word noun phrases ending in Act, Department, Service, Administration, Commission, Bureau, Office, Corporation, or Authority)
Entities are extracted identically from your predicted_summary. F1 is computed over the combined set of (category, entity-string) tuples. Perfect recall of all reference entities plus no spurious added entities yields 1.0.

TFIDF Cosine (weight 0.15) — TF-IDF vector cosine similarity between predicted and reference summaries, fitted on the full evaluation corpus. Captures paraphrased content overlap that ROUGE-L misses.

Structure Compliance (weight 0.10) — 1.0 if the predicted summary falls in [50, 400] words and both fields are non-empty; partial credit proportional to how close a too-short or too-long output gets to the range. Prevents token-dump and empty-output gaming.

Score range: 0.0 to 1.0. Higher is better. Copying the first 150 words of the source document as the summary scores roughly 0.15. Zero-shot LLM summarization without entity guidance scores around 0.40–0.50. A well-tuned model with explicit entity-preservation scores in the 0.60–0.75 range.

Submission Format
Submit a CSV at ./working/submission.csv with exactly these columns:

Column	Type	Notes
id	string	must match an id in test.csv
predicted_title	string	short document title, ≤ 100 characters recommended
predicted_summary	string	plain-language summary, target 50–400 words
Example (the first line is the required header row):

id,predicted_title,predicted_summary
BIL_ab12cd34ef56,"Example Funding Measure","The document authorizes $250,000,000 for fiscal year 2026 under the Example Statutory Programme, directs the Example Coordinating Agency to establish grant programs..."
BIL_cd34ef56gh78,"Example Tax Relief Measure","The document amends Section 44 of the Example Revenue Code to increase the deduction cap for small enterprises..."
Requirements:

One row per test id. Missing rows receive zero credit for that document across all five metric components.
predicted_title and predicted_summary must be non-empty strings.
Escape newlines or quotes inside the summary correctly (CSV standard quoting).
predicted_summary length is not strictly enforced but falling outside [50, 400] words loses the structure-compliance component.
Dataset
public/train.csv — training set with full documents, reference titles, and reference summaries (one row per document).
public/test.csv — test documents with text only; reference title and summary are withheld.
public/sample_submission.csv — example submission with a naive-first-N-words baseline.
Columns
Columns in train.csv:

id (string) — identifier of the form BIL_xxxxxxxxxxxx (opaque hash, no correspondence to any public catalogue).
text (string) — the full document text with its identifying preamble removed.
title (string) — human-written short title for the document.
summary (string) — human-written plain-language summary produced by domain experts.
doc_length_words (int) — whitespace-tokenised word count of the document text, convenience field.
Columns in test.csv:

id (string) — identifier matching sample_submission.csv.
text (string) — the document text to summarize.
doc_length_words (int) — document length in words.
Note: neither title nor summary is disclosed for the test documents.
