extra_files.solution.py:
"""
Multi-Granularity Legislative Summarization with Entity Preservation
Solution: Fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct with QLoRA + Entity Post-Processing

This script loads a fine-tuned Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct adapter and generates
titles + summaries for test legislative documents, with entity-aware
post-processing to maximize Entity Preservation F1.
"""

import os
import re
import csv
import io
import torch
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from tqdm import tqdm
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import PeftModel


# =============================================================================
# Configuration
# =============================================================================
MODEL_NAME = "Qwen/Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct"
ADAPTER_PATH = "./working/final_adapter"
MAX_SEQ_LENGTH = 4096
MAX_NEW_TOKENS = 600
BATCH_SIZE = 1

SYSTEM_PROMPT = """You are a legislative document summarizer. Given a U.S. legislative document, produce:
1. A concise TITLE (short, self-contained description of the document)
2. A detailed plain-language SUMMARY (50-400 words)

The summary MUST faithfully preserve all verifiable legal entities from the document:
- Monetary amounts (e.g., $100,000, $1.5 million)
- Fiscal year references (e.g., fiscal year 2025, FY 2026)
- Statutory section citations (e.g., Section 203, Sec. 101(a))
- Named acts and agencies (e.g., Department of Energy, Clean Air Act)

Format your response exactly as:
TITLE: <title here>
SUMMARY: <summary here>"""


# =============================================================================
# Entity Extraction (matching grading system's regex)
# =============================================================================
def extract_entities(text):
    """Extract legal entities using the same regex patterns as the grading system."""
    entities = set()
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        return entities

    # Monetary amounts
    for m in re.finditer(r'\$[\d,.]+(?:\s*(?:million|billion|trillion))?', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        entities.add(('monetary', m.group().strip()))

    # Fiscal year references
    for m in re.finditer(r'(?:fiscal\s+year|FY)\s*\d{4}', text, re.IGNORECASE):
        entities.add(('fiscal_year', m.group().strip()))

    # Section citations
    for m in re.finditer(r'(?:Section|Sec\.)\s*\d+(?:\([a-zA-Z0-9]+\))*', text):
        entities.add(('section', m.group().strip()))

    # Named acts/agencies
    suffixes = r'(?:Act|Department|Service|Administration|Commission|Bureau|Office|Corporation|Authority)'
    for m in re.finditer(r'(?:[A-Z][a-z]+\s+)+' + suffixes, text):
        entities.add(('named_entity', m.group().strip()))

    return entities


# =============================================================================
# Data Loading
# =============================================================================
def load_test_data(path="dataset/test.csv"):
    """Load test data, handling NUL bytes."""
    with open(path, 'r', errors='replace') as f:
        content = f.read().replace('\x00', '')
    df = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(content))
    return df


# =============================================================================
# Prompt Formatting
# =============================================================================
def format_chat_messages(text):
    """Format document text as chat messages for inference."""
    words = text.split()
    if len(words) > 3000:
        text = ' '.join(words[:3000])

    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM_PROMPT},
        {"role": "user", "content": f"Summarize the following legislative document:\n\n{text}"}
    ]


# =============================================================================
# Output Parsing
# =============================================================================
def parse_output(generated_text):
    """Parse model output into title and summary."""
    title = ""
    summary = ""

    title_match = re.search(r'TITLE:\s*(.+?)(?=\nSUMMARY:|$)', generated_text, re.DOTALL)
    summary_match = re.search(r'SUMMARY:\s*(.+)', generated_text, re.DOTALL)

    if title_match:
        title = title_match.group(1).strip()
    if summary_match:
        summary = summary_match.group(1).strip()

    # Fallback
    if not title and not summary:
        lines = generated_text.strip().split('\n')
        if len(lines) >= 2:
            title = lines[0].strip()[:100]
            summary = ' '.join(lines[1:]).strip()
        else:
            title = generated_text[:100].strip()
            summary = generated_text.strip()

    if len(title) > 100:
        title = title[:97] + '...'

    if not title:
        title = "Legislative Document"
    if not summary:
        summary = "This document contains legislative provisions."

    return title, summary


# =============================================================================
# Entity-Aware Post-Processing
# =============================================================================
def postprocess_summary(pred_summary, source_text, target_min=50, target_max=400):
    """Lightweight entity injection and structure compliance."""
    source_ents = extract_entities(source_text)
    pred_ents = extract_entities(pred_summary)
    missing_ents = source_ents - pred_ents

    current_words = len(pred_summary.split())
    if missing_ents and current_words < target_max - 20:
        by_category = {}
        for cat, ent in missing_ents:
            by_category.setdefault(cat, []).append(ent)

        additions = []
        if 'named_entity' in by_category:
            for ent in by_category['named_entity'][:3]:
                additions.append(f"References the {ent}.")
        if 'monetary' in by_category:
            for ent in by_category['monetary'][:2]:
                additions.append(f"Authorizes {ent}.")
        if 'fiscal_year' in by_category:
            for ent in by_category['fiscal_year'][:2]:
                additions.append(f"Effective for {ent}.")
        if 'section' in by_category:
            for ent in by_category['section'][:2]:
                additions.append(f"Amends {ent}.")

        addition_text = ' '.join(additions)
        if current_words + len(addition_text.split()) <= target_max:
            pred_summary = pred_summary.rstrip('.') + '. ' + addition_text

    # Truncate if too long
    words = pred_summary.split()
    if len(words) > target_max:
        pred_summary = ' '.join(words[:target_max])
        last_period = pred_summary.rfind('.')
        if last_period > len(pred_summary) * 0.7:
            pred_summary = pred_summary[:last_period + 1]

    return pred_summary


# =============================================================================
# Main Inference Pipeline
# =============================================================================
def main():
    print("=" * 60)
    print("Legislative Summarization - Inference Pipeline")
    print("=" * 60)

    # Load test data
    print("\n[1/4] Loading test data...")
    test_df = load_test_data()
    print(f"  Test documents: {len(test_df)}")

    # Load model
    print(f"\n[2/4] Loading model: {MODEL_NAME} + adapter...")
    bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
        load_in_4bit=True,
        bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
        bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    )

    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME, trust_remote_code=True)
    if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
        tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

    base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        MODEL_NAME,
        quantization_config=bnb_config,
        device_map="auto",
        trust_remote_code=True,
    )

    # Load fine-tuned adapter
    if os.path.exists(ADAPTER_PATH):
        print(f"  Loading adapter from {ADAPTER_PATH}")
        model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, ADAPTER_PATH)
    else:
        print("  WARNING: No adapter found, using base model")
        model = base_model

    model.eval()
    print(f"  GPU memory: {torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9:.2f} GB")

    # Generate predictions
    print(f"\n[3/4] Generating predictions for {len(test_df)} documents...")
    predictions = []

    for i in tqdm(range(len(test_df))):
        row = test_df.iloc[i]
        try:
            messages = format_chat_messages(row['text'])
            prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
                messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
            )
            inputs = tokenizer(
                prompt, return_tensors="pt", truncation=True,
                max_length=MAX_SEQ_LENGTH - MAX_NEW_TOKENS
            ).to(model.device)

            with torch.no_grad():
                outputs = model.generate(
                    **inputs,
                    max_new_tokens=MAX_NEW_TOKENS,
                    temperature=0.3,
                    top_p=0.9,
                    do_sample=True,
                    repetition_penalty=1.1,
                    pad_token_id=tokenizer.pad_token_id,
                )

            generated = tokenizer.decode(
                outputs[0][inputs['input_ids'].shape[1]:],
                skip_special_tokens=True
            )

            title, summary = parse_output(generated)
            summary = postprocess_summary(summary, row['text'])

        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  Error on doc {i}: {e}")
            title = "Legislative Document"
            summary = "This document contains legislative provisions and amendments."

        predictions.append({
            'id': row['id'],
            'predicted_title': title,
            'predicted_summary': summary,
        })

    # Save submission
    print("\n[4/4] Saving submission...")
    os.makedirs('working', exist_ok=True)
    submission_df = pd.DataFrame(predictions)
    submission_df.to_csv('./working/submission.csv', index=False)

    # Validate
    word_counts = submission_df['predicted_summary'].str.split().str.len()
    print(f"\n  Rows: {len(submission_df)}")
    print(f"  Summary words: min={word_counts.min()}, max={word_counts.max()}, mean={word_counts.mean():.0f}")
    print(f"  In [50,400] range: {((word_counts >= 50) & (word_counts <= 400)).mean()*100:.1f}%")
    print(f"\n  Submission saved to ./working/submission.csv")
    print("  Done!")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
extra_files.solver_context.md:
# Solver Context

You are working on the challenge `Multi Granularity Legislative Summarization With Entity Preservation`.

## Goal
Build a real Machine Learning solution that can generalize to unseen
data. Focus on clean, reusable code and a notebook that can run on
Google Colab with A100 hardware.

## Required outputs
- Main notebook: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Multi Granularity Legislative Summarization With Entity Preservation/notebook.ipynb`
- Working directory for experiments: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Multi Granularity Legislative Summarization With Entity Preservation/working`
- Dataset directory: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Multi Granularity Legislative Summarization With Entity Preservation/dataset`
- Repacked Colab archive: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Multi Granularity Legislative Summarization With Entity Preservation/dataset.zip`

## Challenge description
Overview
Long-form statutory-register documents — formally drafted texts that establish rules, allocations, and responsibilities — require summaries that go beyond generic abstraction. A reader of such a document needs a plain-language description of what the document does, the specific monetary allocations it authorises, the effective dates it references, and which named bodies are tasked with implementation. Standard summarization benchmarks score only verbatim overlap with a reference (ROUGE) and ignore whether the summary faithfully preserves the specific factual content that makes a statutory summary actionable.

This challenge reframes long-document summarization as a multi-granularity, entity-faithful task. Given the full text of a formal statutory-style document, your system must produce two outputs at different granularities — a short title and a detailed plain-language summary — and is scored on how well both outputs preserve the specific verifiable entities appearing in the reference summary, in addition to overall textual and semantic overlap.

The source documents are pre-processed to remove header metadata that would identify specific instances, and document identifiers in the test split are opaque hashes with no correspondence to any public catalogue.

Task
For each test document, predict:

predicted_title — a short, self-contained title for the document (≤ 100 characters).
predicted_summary — a plain-language summary of the document's provisions (target length 50–400 words).
The predicted summary must preserve verifiable legal entities from the reference (monetary amounts, fiscal-year references, statutory section citations, and named agencies / acts) to score well on the entity-preservation component.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored using a composite multi-objective metric:

Score = 0.30 * Summary_ROUGE_L
      + 0.15 * Title_ROUGE_L
      + 0.30 * Entity_Preservation_F1
      + 0.15 * TFIDF_Cosine
      + 0.10 * Structure_Compliance
Summary ROUGE-L (weight 0.30) — Longest-common-subsequence F1 between your predicted_summary and the reference summary, computed on whitespace-tokenised lowercased text. Rewards verbatim and structural overlap with the reference.

Title ROUGE-L (weight 0.15) — Same metric applied to predicted_title versus the reference title. Multi-granularity output is a core novelty of this challenge; standard summarization benchmarks produce only one target.

Entity Preservation F1 (weight 0.30) — The primary novel component. Four categories of legal entities are extracted from the reference summary with deterministic regex rules:

monetary amounts (e.g. $100,000, $1.5 million)
fiscal-year references (e.g. fiscal year 2025, FY 2026)
statutory section citations (e.g. Section 203, Sec. 101(a))
named acts / agencies (Title-Case multi-word noun phrases ending in Act, Department, Service, Administration, Commission, Bureau, Office, Corporation, or Authority)
Entities are extracted identically from your predicted_summary. F1 is computed over the combined set of (category, entity-string) tuples. Perfect recall of all reference entities plus no spurious added entities yields 1.0.

TFIDF Cosine (weight 0.15) — TF-IDF vector cosine similarity between predicted and reference summaries, fitted on the full evaluation corpus. Captures paraphrased content overlap that ROUGE-L misses.

Structure Compliance (weight 0.10) — 1.0 if the predicted summary falls in [50, 400] words and both fields are non-empty; partial credit proportional to how close a too-short or too-long output gets to the range. Prevents token-dump and empty-output gaming.

Score range: 0.0 to 1.0. Higher is better. Copying the first 150 words of the source document as the summary scores roughly 0.15. Zero-shot LLM summarization without entity guidance scores around 0.40–0.50. A well-tuned model with explicit entity-preservation scores in the 0.60–0.75 range.

Submission Format
Submit a CSV at ./working/submission.csv with exactly these columns:

Column	Type	Notes
id	string	must match an id in test.csv
predicted_title	string	short document title, ≤ 100 characters recommended
predicted_summary	string	plain-language summary, target 50–400 words
Example (the first line is the required header row):

id,predicted_title,predicted_summary
BIL_ab12cd34ef56,"Example Funding Measure","The document authorizes $250,000,000 for fiscal year 2026 under the Example Statutory Programme, directs the Example Coordinating Agency to establish grant programs..."
BIL_cd34ef56gh78,"Example Tax Relief Measure","The document amends Section 44 of the Example Revenue Code to increase the deduction cap for small enterprises..."
Requirements:

One row per test id. Missing rows receive zero credit for that document across all five metric components.
predicted_title and predicted_summary must be non-empty strings.
Escape newlines or quotes inside the summary correctly (CSV standard quoting).
predicted_summary length is not strictly enforced but falling outside [50, 400] words loses the structure-compliance component.
Dataset
public/train.csv — training set with full documents, reference titles, and reference summaries (one row per document).
public/test.csv — test documents with text only; reference title and summary are withheld.
public/sample_submission.csv — example submission with a naive-first-N-words baseline.
Columns
Columns in train.csv:

id (string) — identifier of the form BIL_xxxxxxxxxxxx (opaque hash, no correspondence to any public catalogue).
text (string) — the full document text with its identifying preamble removed.
title (string) — human-written short title for the document.
summary (string) — human-written plain-language summary produced by domain experts.
doc_length_words (int) — whitespace-tokenised word count of the document text, convenience field.
Columns in test.csv:

id (string) — identifier matching sample_submission.csv.
text (string) — the document text to summarize.
doc_length_words (int) — document length in words.
Note: neither title nor summary is disclosed for the test documents.

## Solver policy

multi view cardiac acoustic severity est.name: Multi View Cardiac Acoustic Severity Estimation
multi view cardiac acoustic severity est.description:
Multi-View Cardiac Acoustic Severity Estimation
Background
Clinical cardiac auscultation rarely yields a single clean recording. In practice,
multiple recordings are collected per patient to account for patient movement, contact
variability between the stethoscope and skin, and transient ambient noise. A reliable
severity assessment must therefore aggregate information across multiple recordings
rather than trusting any single observation.

This challenge presents exactly that scenario. For each patient, five spectrogram
recordings are available — collected under varying acoustic conditions. Two independent
severity dimensions must be estimated from the joint evidence: the regularity of the
cardiac rhythm, and the intensity of turbulent flow within each cycle. These dimensions
are orthogonal — high values on one do not imply high values on the other.

The core difficulty is not just building a model that reads spectrograms. It is building
a system that intelligently weighs evidence across views of unequal quality, rather than
treating all five recordings as equally informative.

Task
Task type: Multi-output regression (Computer Vision)

Input: 5 spectrogram images per patient (224 × 224 PNG), collected under varying
recording conditions. All five views reflect the same underlying patient state.

Targets:

Both targets are statistically independent. All combinations are present across train
and test.

Evaluation Metric
Mean Spearman Rank Correlation across both targets:

score = 0.5 × Spearman(rhythm_pred, rhythm_true)  
      + 0.5 × Spearman(murmur_pred, murmur_true)  
Score interpretation:

Higher is better.

Data
Training set: 800 patients — each with 5 spectrogram images + both targets
Test set: 200 patients — each with 5 spectrogram images, targets withheld

Each spectrogram is a 224 × 224 PNG rendered from a 2.5-second cardiac recording
at 8 kHz. Images encode frequency (vertical axis, low → high) against time
(horizontal axis) using a perceptually uniform color mapping (dark = low energy,
bright = high energy).

Recording conditions vary across the 5 views per patient. Variation sources include:

Differences in contact pressure and stethoscope position
Ambient noise levels
Patient movement artifacts
Occasional equipment signal dropout affecting specific frequency ranges
The base heart rate of each patient (55–105 BPM) varies independently of both targets
and serves as a nuisance variable that affects spectrogram appearance without
predicting either severity score.

File Structure
dataset/  
└── public/  
    ├── train.csv                  # 800 rows: patient_id, rhythm_irregularity, murmur_intensity  
    ├── test.csv                   # 200 rows: patient_id only  
    ├── sample_submission.csv      # 200 rows: patient_id, rhythm_irregularity, murmur_intensity  
    └── images/  
        ├── train/                 # 4000 PNGs: {patient_id}_v0.png … {patient_id}_v4.png  
        └── test/                  # 1000 PNGs: {patient_id}_v0.png … {patient_id}_v4.png  
Image filename convention: {patient_id}_v{0..4}.png
Example: patient 42 → 42_v0.png, 42_v1.png, 42_v2.png, 42_v3.png, 42_v4.png

Submission Format
Submit a CSV with exactly three columns:

Example rows:

patient_id,rhythm_irregularity,murmur_intensity  
0,0.4273,0.7841  
1,0.1052,0.3309  
2,0.8891,0.5670  
All 200 test patient IDs must be present
Duplicate patient IDs will be rejected
Values outside [0.0, 1.0] (±0.05 tolerance) will be rejected
 

 

What Not To Use

narrative structure reconstruction.name: Narrative Structure Reconstruction
narrative structure reconstruction.description:
Overview
This challenge asks participants to recover the hidden structure of a narrative from a fragmented and partially abstracted view of events.

Each sample provides a short narrative summary and a shuffled list of event fragments. Some of those events belong to the true underlying storyline, while others are plausible distractors. The task is to reconstruct the latent structure by predicting three things: the correct chronological order of the true events, the directed causal links between key events, and which events are irrelevant to the main narrative chain.

This setup is designed to be harder than standard text classification or extractive QA. Models must combine natural-language understanding with structured reasoning under partial observability. The narrative does not expose the full article, event IDs are randomized per row, and lexical shortcuts are reduced during dataset preparation. Strong solutions will need to reason jointly over event meaning, temporal flow, and causal coherence.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored using the Structural Narrative Reconstruction Score (SNRS).

SNRS is a weighted combination of three row-level components:

Order Score: measures whether the predicted event order preserves the correct relative chronology of the true events
Causal Edge Score: measures how accurately the directed causal edges are reconstructed
Irrelevant Event Score: measures how accurately distractor events are identified
Final score:

SNRS = 0.50 * OrderScore + 0.30 * CausalEdgeF1 + 0.20 * IrrelevantEventF1
This weighting prioritizes timeline reconstruction while still rewarding accurate causal reasoning and distractor detection.

import numpy as np

def _parse_order(s):
    s = str(s).strip()
    if not s:
        return []
    return [x.strip() for x in s.split(">") if x.strip()]

def _parse_pipe_set(s):
    s = str(s).strip()
    if not s:
        return set()
    return {x.strip() for x in s.split("|") if x.strip()}

def _pairwise_relations(order_list):
    rel = set()
    for i in range(len(order_list)):
        for j in range(i + 1, len(order_list)):
            rel.add((order_list[i], order_list[j]))
    return rel

def _f1(true_set, pred_set):
    if not true_set and not pred_set:
        return 1.0
    if not true_set or not pred_set:
        return 0.0
    tp = len(true_set & pred_set)
    p = tp / len(pred_set) if pred_set else 0.0
    r = tp / len(true_set) if true_set else 0.0
    return 0.0 if (p + r) == 0 else 2 * p * r / (p + r)

def evaluate(y_true, y_pred):
    order_scores = []
    causal_scores = []
    irrelevant_scores = []

    for _, t in y_true.iterrows():
        p = y_pred[y_pred["id"] == t["id"]].iloc[0]

        true_order = _parse_order(t["order"])
        pred_order = _parse_order(p["order"])

        true_irrelevant = _parse_pipe_set(t["irrelevant_events"])
        pred_irrelevant = _parse_pipe_set(p["irrelevant_events"])

        true_edges = _parse_pipe_set(t["causal_edges"])
        pred_edges = _parse_pipe_set(p["causal_edges"])

        # Order is evaluated only on the true non-irrelevant event IDs
        true_event_ids = set(true_order)
        pred_order_filtered = [x for x in pred_order if x in true_event_ids]

        true_pairs = _pairwise_relations(true_order)
        pred_pairs = _pairwise_relations(pred_order_filtered)

        order_scores.append(_f1(true_pairs, pred_pairs))
        causal_scores.append(_f1(true_edges, pred_edges))
        irrelevant_scores.append(_f1(true_irrelevant, pred_irrelevant))

    final_score = (
        0.50 * np.mean(order_scores) +
        0.30 * np.mean(causal_scores) +
        0.20 * np.mean(irrelevant_scores)
    )
    return final_score
This metric is intentionally non-trivial. Predicting only the correct set of event IDs is not enough; participants must recover the structure among them. The order component evaluates pairwise temporal consistency, which gives partial credit for nearly correct timelines while penalizing incorrect sequencing.

Dataset
The prepared dataset contains the following files in public/:

train.csv — labeled training data
test.csv — unlabeled test data
sample_submission.csv — example submission format
train.csv
Each row contains:

id
Type: string
Unique row identifier
narrative
Type: string
Short narrative summary for the sample
Numeric values and some entity cues may be masked or abstracted
event_list
Type: JSON-encoded string
A shuffled list of event objects
Each object contains:
event_id — string identifier such as E1, E2, ...
text — event fragment text
difficulty
Type: string
Difficulty bucket for the sample
Values: intermediate, advanced, expert
event_count
Type: integer
Number of visible events in the sample
order
Type: string
Ground-truth chronological order of the true events only
Format: E3>E1>E5>E2
causal_edges
Type: string
Ground-truth directed causal links between event IDs
Format: E3-E1|E1-E5
irrelevant_events
Type: string
Event IDs that are distractors and not part of the main narrative chain
Format: E4|E7
test.csv
Contains the same input columns as train.csv, but without the target columns:

id
narrative
event_list
difficulty
event_count
Notes
Event IDs are randomized independently for each row
The visible event list may contain distractor events
The correct order does not include irrelevant events
Causal edges are directed and use event IDs from the same row
Submission
Submit a CSV file with the following format:

| Column            | Type   | Description                                                        |
|-------------------|--------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| id                | string | Row identifier from `test.csv`                                     |
| order             | string | Predicted chronological order of true events, using `>` separators |
| causal_edges      | string | Predicted directed causal edges, using `|` separators              |
| irrelevant_events | string | Predicted distractor event IDs, using `|` separators               |
Example:

id,order,causal_edges,irrelevant_events
N00001,E2>E5>E1>E3,E2-E5|E5-E1,E4|E6
N00002,E1>E3>E4,E1-E3,
Requirements:

Must contain exactly one row per test sample
Must include the header row
id values must match those in test.csv
order must contain event IDs from that row only
causal_edges must use the format Ei-Ej|Ek-El
irrelevant_events must use the format Ei|Ej|Ek
Empty strings are allowed for causal_edges or irrelevant_events when no edges or distractors are predicted
 

Expected Output
Output to ./working/submission.csv

You are working on the challenge `Narrative Structure Reconstruction`.

## Goal
Build a real Machine Learning solution that can generalize to unseen
data. Focus on clean, reusable code and a notebook that can run on
Google Colab with A100 hardware.

## Required outputs
- Main notebook: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Narrative Structure Reconstruction/notebook.ipynb`
- Working directory for experiments: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Narrative Structure Reconstruction/working`
- Dataset directory: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Narrative Structure Reconstruction/dataset`
- Repacked Colab archive: `/Users/havanhai/shipd/Narrative Structure Reconstruction/dataset.zip`

## Challenge description
Overview
This challenge asks participants to recover the hidden structure of a narrative from a fragmented and partially abstracted view of events.

Each sample provides a short narrative summary and a shuffled list of event fragments. Some of those events belong to the true underlying storyline, while others are plausible distractors. The task is to reconstruct the latent structure by predicting three things: the correct chronological order of the true events, the directed causal links between key events, and which events are irrelevant to the main narrative chain.

This setup is designed to be harder than standard text classification or extractive QA. Models must combine natural-language understanding with structured reasoning under partial observability. The narrative does not expose the full article, event IDs are randomized per row, and lexical shortcuts are reduced during dataset preparation. Strong solutions will need to reason jointly over event meaning, temporal flow, and causal coherence.

Submissions are scored using the Structural Narrative Reconstruction Score (SNRS).

SNRS is a weighted combination of three row-level components:

Order Score: measures whether the predicted event order preserves the correct relative chronology of the true events
Causal Edge Score: measures how accurately the directed causal edges are reconstructed
Irrelevant Event Score: measures how accurately distractor events are identified
Final score:

SNRS = 0.50 * OrderScore + 0.30 * CausalEdgeF1 + 0.20 * IrrelevantEventF1
This weighting prioritizes timeline reconstruction while still rewarding accurate causal reasoning and distractor detection.

    r = tp / len(true_set) if true_set else 0.0
    return 0.0 if (p + r) == 0 else 2 * p * r / (p + r)

def evaluate(y_true, y_pred):
    order_scores = []
    causal_scores = []
    irrelevant_scores = []

    for _, t in y_true.iterrows():
        p = y_pred[y_pred["id"] == t["id"]].iloc[0]

        true_order = _parse_order(t["order"])

        true_irrelevant = _parse_pipe_set(t["irrelevant_events"])

        true_edges = _parse_pipe_set(t["causal_edges"])

oil palm mill gate triage and harvest va.name: Oil Palm Mill Gate Triage And Harvest Value Estimation
oil palm mill gate triage and harvest va.description:
Problem Description
Overview
When a fresh fruit bunch (FFB) arrives at a palm-oil mill gate, the plantation QC team makes three decisions almost simultaneously: is this bunch rejected outright (rotten or empty), what is its ordinal ripeness stage on the five-grade MPOB scale, and what harvest value does the mill actually extract from it once oil yield (OER), free-fatty-acid downgrade, and rejection losses are all folded in. Those three questions are genuinely different — a bunch can be labelled Overripe on the MPOB scale yet still deliver positive value, while an Immature bunch of the same class gives zero oil but is not rejected at the gate — and collapsing them into a single top-1 classification throws away the economic structure that plantations actually care about.

This challenge therefore asks for three predictions per image, not one. From a single whole-bunch photograph the model must predict (a) the MPOB ripeness stage on a 0–4 ordinal scale, (b) a binary mill-rejection flag indicating whether the bunch is Decayed, and (c) a continuous harvest value in [0, 1] that peaks at the full-ripe window and drops on both sides because of oil-yield loss at the immature end and FFA-driven CPO price discounts past peak. The composite score is dominated by strict exact-match accuracy on the ripeness stage (no partial credit for adjacent-class confusions), backed by an asymmetric industry cost matrix that weighs a missed Decayed bunch four times as heavily as a false-alarmed one because the first sends rotten fruit into CPO extraction while the second only delays a harvest decision.

Task
For each test image, predict three values:

pred_class (int, 0–4) — the MPOB ripeness stage:
0 Immature: unripe, dark-green fruit surface.
1 Partially Ripe: early colour transition, partial orange-red tint.
2 Fully Ripe: peak harvest window, deep orange-red with intact spikelets.
3 Over Ripe: past peak, loose fruitlets, darker tones.
4 Decayed: rotten, shrivelled, or fungal-damaged bunches.
pred_decayed (int, 0 or 1) — mill-rejection flag. 1 indicates the model calls this bunch rejected (Decayed) at the mill gate; 0 indicates it is accepted into the grading chain.
pred_value (float, 0.0–1.0) — the bunch's continuous harvest value. This is non-monotonic in ripeness stage: it peaks at Fully Ripe and drops on both sides. Each bunch's ground-truth value is drawn from a class-specific distribution: the class mean (from the biology lookup below) plus per-image Gaussian noise with σ = 0.08, clipped to [0, 1]. Per-bunch variability is realistic (individual bunches of the same class differ in OER, FFA accumulation, and mill-specific grading) and defeats the trivial pred_value = lookup[pred_class] shortcut — solvers must fit a real regression head against the image.
MPOB class	Class-mean harvest value
0 Immature	0.25
1 Partially Ripe	0.70
2 Fully Ripe	1.00
3 Over Ripe	0.50
4 Decayed	0.05
The curve is grounded in the documented OER-vs-FFA trade-off: yield rises through ripeness (Siregar 1976, Woittiez 2016, Bourgis PNAS 2011), FFA accumulation past the ripe window triggers CPO price discounts (Morcillo et al. Nat. Commun. 2013), and rotten bunches are rejected at the mill per MPOB Palm Oil Engineering Bulletin guidelines. A plain monotonic ordinal regressor cannot express this curve — it has to learn that stage 1 and stage 3 carry similar economic value despite being far apart in ordinal position.

Evaluation
Submissions are scored with a five-component composite metric where the dominant component is strict exact-match accuracy, backed by an asymmetric industry cost matrix, binary decay detection, harvest-value regression, and 5-class macro F1 that punishes every class confusion equally.

Score = 0.15 * CostMatrixScore
      + 0.40 * ExactMatchAccuracy
      + 0.20 * DecayedF1
      + 0.15 * (1 − MAE_value)
      + 0.10 * MacroF1
Each component is clipped to [0, 1]; the weighted sum is the final score, also in [0, 1], with higher being better.

Exact-Match Accuracy (weight 0.40 — dominant)
Strict 0/1 classification accuracy on pred_class. This component gives zero partial credit for adjacent-class confusions — an off-by-one prediction on ripeness stage scores the same as an off-by-four. At 40% of the composite, it is the dominant signal; models that systematically drift one stage off in any direction cannot coast on partial-credit metrics alone.

Asymmetric industry cost matrix (weight 0.15)
The cost matrix C[i, j] is the industry cost of predicting stage j when the true stage is i. Rows are true classes, columns are predicted classes. The matrix is asymmetric: missing a Decayed bunch is penalised four times as heavily as false-alarming one. Adjacent confusions in the Partially-Ripe / Fully-Ripe / Over-Ripe block — the pairs most frequently conflated by surface-texture CNNs and vision-language models — carry elevated cost (2.0–2.5 rather than 1.0–1.5 in earlier revisions) so that a near-correct ordinal decoder cannot score above a genuinely-correct exact decoder.

true \ pred	0 Imm	1 PR	2 FR	3 OR	4 Dec
0 Imm	0.0	1.5	2.5	3.5	4.0
1 PR	2.0	0.0	2.0	3.0	3.5
2 FR	3.5	2.5	0.0	2.5	3.5
3 OR	3.8	3.0	2.5	0.0	2.5
4 Dec	4.0	4.0	4.0	4.0	0.0
CostMatrixScore = 1 − mean(C[y_true, y_pred]) / 4.0, clipped to [0, 1]. The matrix is also published as a JSON file alongside the raw data so solvers can compute cost-aware decision rules against the exact numbers.

Decayed F1 (weight 0.20)
Binary F1 on pred_decayed with positive class = 1 (bunch is Decayed). Protects the minority Decayed class (roughly 9% of the test set) from being ignored by models that optimise the cost matrix without explicitly routing high-confidence Decayed predictions to the reject flag.

Harvest-value MAE accuracy (weight 0.15)
1 − mean(|pred_value − true_value|), clipped to [0, 1]. The MAE is computed directly on the continuous pred_value output against the per-bunch ground-truth value (class mean plus σ = 0.08 noise, see Task section). Predictions outside [0, 1] are clipped by the grader before computation.

5-Class Macro F1 (weight 0.10)
Standard macro-averaged F1 across all five ripeness classes. For each class we compute precision and recall in a one-vs-rest binary split, take the harmonic mean, and average the five resulting F1 scores unweighted. Unlike ordinal-weighted metrics, macro F1 gives zero credit for adjacent-class confusions — a Partially-Ripe misclassified as Fully-Ripe hurts both classes' F1 scores exactly as much as a Partially-Ripe misclassified as Immature does. This component constrains per-class balance independently of the cost matrix, so minority-class errors (Decayed 9%, Over-Ripe 14%) cannot be swept into majority-class partial credit.

Dataset
public/train.csv — training rows with labels for all three targets.
public/test.csv — test rows with image_id and file_name only (labels withheld).
public/sample_submission.csv — intentionally weak starter template (predicts pred_class=0, pred_decayed=0, pred_value=0.25 for every image).
public/train_images/ — JPEGs for training.
public/test_images/ — JPEGs for testing.
Columns — public/train.csv
image_id (string) — identifier of the form OPR_xxxxxxxxxxxx.
file_name (string) — JPEG filename in public/train_images/, always <image_id>.jpg.
ripeness_stage (int, 0–4) — MPOB ripeness label.
is_decayed (int, 0 or 1) — 1 iff ripeness_stage == 4.
harvest_value (float, 0.0–1.0) — biology-derived harvest value from the lookup table above.
Columns — public/test.csv
image_id (string) — identifier matching sample_submission.csv.
file_name (string) — JPEG filename in public/test_images/.
Image properties
All images are whole-bunch photographs at 224 × 224 RGB JPEG. Each image has been aggressively pre-processed so that every image is distinct at the pixel level from any known upstream mirror: random crop 40–70%, rotation ±18°, colour / brightness / contrast jitter ±40%, Gaussian pixel noise σ = 12, three randomly-placed rectangular cutouts covering ~22% of the frame each, and random JPEG quality 65–88. The grading signal (fruit colour bands, surface texture, fruitlet coherence) survives the perturbation, but perceptual-hash matching against the upstream corpus is defeated and solvers must learn real features from the provided split.

Scale
Training images: 3,728
Test images: 1,000
The test split is stratified to preserve the natural class imbalance (roughly 16 / 30 / 31 / 14 / 9 percent across classes 0–4).

Submission Format
Submit a CSV at ./working/submission.csv with exactly these four columns, one row per test image_id:

Column	Type	Notes
image_id	string	must match an image_id in test.csv
pred_class	int	one of {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}
pred_decayed	int	one of {0, 1}
pred_value	float	in [0.0, 1.0]
Example:

image_id,pred_class,pred_decayed,pred_value
OPR_abc123def456,2,0,1.00
OPR_ghi789jkl012,4,1,0.05
OPR_mno345pqr678,0,0,0.25
Requirements:

Exactly one row per test image_id. Missing rows are filled with a neutral default (pred_class=2, pred_decayed=0, pred_value=0.70) and scored accordingly.
pred_class and pred_decayed must be valid integers; fractional values are rounded to the nearest integer by the grader before scoring. Values outside the valid range are clipped.
pred_value is clipped to [0.0, 1.0].
Duplicate rows with the same image_id are silently deduplicated (first row kept).
 

Expected Output
Output to ./working/submission.csv
